01-01.text: The original American Telephone & Telegraph Company is divested of its 22 Bell System companies as a result of the settlement of the 1974 United States Department of Justice antitrust suit against AT&T.
Alcatraz Island becomes a United States federal prison.
01-01.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-01.text: The United States Post Office Department issues a set of 12 stamps commemorating the 200th anniversary of George Washington's birth.

01-02.text: Georgia becomes the fourth state to ratify the United States Constitution.
United States President Richard Nixon signs a bill lowering the maximum U.S. speed limit to 55 MPH in order to conserve gasoline during an OPEC embargo.
The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) convicts 33 members of a German spy ring headed by Fritz Joubert Duquesne in the largest espionage case in United States history—the Duquesne Spy Ring.
01-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-03.text: Alaska was admitted as the 49th state, changing representation in Congress and the Electoral College.
Frances P. Bolton and her son, Oliver from Ohio, become the first mother and son to serve simultaneously in the U.S. Congress.
American Civil War: Delaware votes not to secede from the United States.

01-04.text: Nancy Pelosi became the first woman to serve as Speaker of the House.
United States President Richard Nixon refuses to hand over materials subpoenaed by the Senate Watergate Committee.
Samuel Colt sells his first revolver pistol to the United States government.
01-04.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-05.text: United States President Richard Nixon orders the development of a Space Shuttle program.
In a speech given to the United States Congress, United States President Dwight D. Eisenhower announces the establishment of what will later be called the Eisenhower Doctrine.
United States President Harry S. Truman unveils his Fair Deal program.

01-06.text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt delivered the Four Freedoms speech to Congress.
01-06.ap_connection: Presidential agenda setting; civil liberties; foreign policy

01-06.text: Congress reconvened after the January 6 attack and certified the Electoral College results.
01-06.ap_connection: Electoral College; peaceful transfer of power; Congress

01-06.text: American Civil Rights Movement: Edgar Ray Killen is arrested as a suspect in the 1964 murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner.
01-06.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

01-07.text: The United States recognizes the new Cuban government of Fidel Castro.
01-07.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-08.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson declares a "War on Poverty" in the United States.
Ella T. Grasso becomes Governor of Connecticut, the first woman to serve as a Governor in the United States other than by succeeding her husband.
01-08.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-08.text: Crazy Horse and his warriors fight their last battle against the United States Cavalry at Wolf Mountain, Montana Territory.
01-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-09.text: Connecticut becomes the fifth state to ratify the Constitution.
World War II: The Sixth United States Army begins the invasion of Lingayen Gulf.
01-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-09.text: American Civil War: "Star of the West" incident occurs near Charleston, South Carolina.
01-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-10.text: Thomas Paine published Common Sense, arguing for American independence and popular sovereignty.
01-10.ap_connection: Popular sovereignty; political participation; founding ideals

01-10.text: Holy See-United States relations: The United States and Holy See (Vatican City) re-establish full diplomatic relations after almost 117 years, overturning the United States Congress's 1867 ban on public funding for such a diplomatic envoy.

Sandinista Daniel Ortega becomes president of Nicaragua and vows to continue the transformation to socialism and alliance with the Soviet Union and Cuba; American policy continues to support the Contras in their revolt against the Nicaraguan government.

01-11.text: American Civil War: Battle of Arkansas Post: General John McClernand and Admiral David Dixon Porter capture the Arkansas River for the Union.
01-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-11.text: American Civil War: CSS Alabama encounters and sinks the USS Hatteras off Galveston Lighthouse in Texas.
01-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-11.text: Illinois Governor George Ryan commutes the death sentences of 167 prisoners on Illinois's death row based on the Jon Burge scandal.
01-11.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-12.text: World War II: United States President Franklin D. Roosevelt creates the National War Labor Board.
Persian Gulf War: An act of the U.S. Congress authorizes the use of American military force to drive Iraq out of Kuwait.
Hattie Caraway becomes the first woman elected to the United States Senate.

01-13.text: United States President Andrew Jackson writes to Vice President Martin Van Buren expressing his opposition to South Carolina's defiance of federal authority in the Nullification Crisis.
U.S. Marines land in Honolulu, Hawaii from the USS Boston to prevent the queen from abrogating the Bayonet Constitution.
Robert C. Weaver becomes the first African American Cabinet member when he is appointed United States Secretary of Housing and Urban Development.

01-14.text: American Revolutionary War: Ratification Day, United States - Congress ratifies the Treaty of Paris with Great Britain.
World War II: Franklin D. Roosevelt becomes the first President of the United States to travel by airplane while in office when he travels from Miami to Morocco to meet with Winston Churchill.
The "Fundamental Orders", the first written constitution that created a government, is adopted in Connecticut.

01-15.text: Martin Luther King Jr. was born; his leadership later helped push federal civil rights action.
01-15.ap_connection: Civil rights movement; social movements; federal legislation

01-15.text: The Pentagon is dedicated in Arlington, Virginia.
01-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-15.text: War of 1812: American frigate USS President, commanded by Commodore Stephen Decatur, is captured by a squadron of four British frigates.

01-16.text: Temperance movement: The United States ratifies the Eighteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, requiring Prohibition in the United States one year after ratification.
US President Bill Clinton awards former President Theodore Roosevelt a posthumous Medal of Honor for his service in the Spanish-American War.
The United States Senate accepts the Anglo-German treaty of 1899 in which the United Kingdom renounces its claims to the Samoan islands.

01-17.text: A group of Modoc warriors defeats the United States Army in the First Battle of the Stronghold, part of the Modoc War.
01-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-17.text: American Revolutionary War: Battle of Cowpens: Continental troops under Brigadier General Daniel Morgan defeat British forces under Lieutenant Colonel Banastre Tarleton at the battle in South Carolina.
Lorrin A. Thurston, along with the Citizens' Committee of Public Safety, led the Overthrow of the Kingdom of Hawaii and the government of Queen Liliʻuokalani.
01-17.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-18.text: Died: John Tyler, American soldier, lawyer, and politician, 10th President of the United States (b. 1790)
Born: Daniel Webster, American lawyer and politician, 14th United States Secretary of State (d. 1852)
01-18.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-18.text: Died: Sargent Shriver, American politician and diplomat, 21st United States Ambassador to France (b. 1915)
01-18.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-19.text: Japan and the United States sign the US-Japan Mutual Security Treaty
01-19.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-19.text: The United States Senate votes against joining the League of Nations.
American Civil War: Battle of Mill Springs: The Confederacy suffers its first significant defeat in the conflict.
01-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-20.text: The first presidential inauguration under the 20th Amendment was held, moving Inauguration Day from March to January.
Donald Trump is inaugurated as the 45th President of the United States of America.
Barack Obama is inaugurated as the 44th President of the United States of America, becoming the first African-American President of the United States.

01-21.text: Over 400 cities across America and 160+ countries worldwide participate in a large-scale women's march, on Donald Trump's first full day as president of the United States.
War on Drugs: In one of the largest drug busts in American history, the United States Coast Guard intercepts a ship with over 4,300 kilograms (9,500 lb) of cocaine on board.
01-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-21.text: American Civil War: Jefferson Davis resigns from the United States Senate.

01-22.text: The Supreme Court decided Roe v. Wade, recognizing a constitutional right to abortion under privacy doctrine.
01-22.ap_connection: Civil liberties; privacy; substantive due process

01-22.text: The Supreme Court of the United States delivers its decisions in Roe v. Wade and Doe v. Bolton, legalizing elective abortion in all fifty states.
01-22.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

01-22.text: World War I: President Woodrow Wilson of the still-neutral United States calls for "peace without victory" in Europe.

01-23.text: The 24th Amendment to the United States Constitution, prohibiting the use of poll taxes in national elections, is ratified.
United States President Richard Nixon announces that a peace accord has been reached in Vietnam.
Charles Lindbergh testifies before the U.S. Congress and recommends that the United States negotiate a neutrality pact with Adolf Hitler.

01-24.text: The 24th Amendment was ratified, banning poll taxes in federal elections.
The 20th Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, changing the beginning and end of terms for all elected federal offices.
In Brushaber v. Union Pacific Railroad Co., the Supreme Court of the United States declares the federal income tax constitutional.
01-24.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

01-25.text: World War II: Thailand declares war on the United States and United Kingdom.
01-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-25.text: Five people are shot outside the CIA Headquarters in Langley, Virginia. Two are killed and three wounded.
01-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-26.text: Lewinsky scandal: On American television, U.S. President Bill Clinton denies having had "sexual relations" with former White House intern Monica Lewinsky.
World War II: The first United States forces arrive in Europe landing in Northern Ireland.
01-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-26.text: American Civil War: General Ambrose Burnside is relieved of command of the Army of the Potomac after the disastrous Fredericksburg campaign. He is replaced by Joseph Hooker.

01-27.text: The U.S. Congress approves Indian Territory (in what is present-day Oklahoma), clearing the way for forced relocation of the Eastern Indians on the "Trail of Tears".

United States, United Kingdom, and Soviet Union sign the Outer Space Treaty in Washington, D.C., banning deployment of nuclear weapons in space, and limiting use of the Moon and other celestial bodies to peaceful purposes.
01-27.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-27.text: American Revolutionary War: Henry Knox's "noble train of artillery" arrives in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

01-28.text: An act of the U.S. Congress creates the United States Coast Guard as a branch of the United States Armed Forces.
United States troops leave Cuba with the exception of Guantanamo Bay Naval Base after being there since the Spanish-American War.
01-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

01-29.text: Governor of Illinois Rod Blagojevich is removed from office following his conviction of several corruption charges, including the alleged solicitation of personal benefit in exchange for an appointment to the United States Senate as a replacement for then-U.S. president-elect Barack Obama.
Charles Curtis of Kansas becomes the first Native American U.S. Senator.
01-29.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

01-30.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt was born; his presidency reshaped expectations of federal power during the New Deal.

In the first assassination attempt against a President of the United States, Richard Lawrence attempts to shoot president Andrew Jackson, but fails and is subdued by a crowd, including several congressmen as well as Jackson himself.
African-American civil rights leader Martin Luther King, Jr.'s home is bombed in retaliation for the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
01-30.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

01-31.text: American Civil War: The United States Congress passes the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, abolishing slavery and submits it to the states for ratification.
United States President Harry S. Truman announces a program to develop the hydrogen bomb.
Vietnam War: Viet Cong guerrillas attack the United States embassy in Saigon, and other attacks, in the early morning hours, later grouped together as the Tet Offensive.
01-31.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-01.text: Four students began the Greensboro sit-ins, a major civil rights protest against segregation.
02-01.ap_connection: Civil rights; linkage institutions; social movements

02-01.text: President Abraham Lincoln signs the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution.
World War II: U.S. Navy conducts Marshalls-Gilberts raids, the first offensive action by the United States against Japanese forces in the Pacific Theater.
02-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-02.text: Born: John Cornyn, American lawyer and politician, 49th Attorney General of Texas
02-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-02.text: Born: James L. Usry, American politician, first African-American mayor of Atlantic City, New Jersey (d. 2002)
02-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-02.text: Born: Curtis Guild, Jr., American journalist and politician, 43rd Governor of Massachusetts (d. 1915)
02-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-03.text: The 15th Amendment was ratified, prohibiting voting discrimination based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude.
02-03.ap_connection: Voting rights; Reconstruction amendments; political participation

02-03.text: The 16th Amendment was ratified, authorizing Congress to collect a federal income tax.
The Fifteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, guaranteeing voting rights to male citizens regardless of race.

02-04.text: George Washington is unanimously elected as the first President of the United States by the U.S. Electoral College.
American Civil War: In Montgomery, Alabama, delegates from six break-away U.S. states meet and form the Confederate States of America.
02-04.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-04.text: John Marshall is sworn in as Chief Justice of the United States.
02-04.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

02-05.text: The Congress of the United States passes the Immigration Act of 1917 over President Woodrow Wilson's veto.
South Carolina becomes the second state to ratify the Articles of Confederation.

02-06.text: Massachusetts becomes the sixth state to ratify the United States Constitution.

In testimony before a United States Senate subcommittee, Lockheed Corporation president Carl Kotchian admits that the company had paid out approximately $3 million in bribes to the office of Japanese Prime Minister Kakuei Tanaka.
Spanish-American War: The Treaty of Paris, a peace treaty between the United States and Spain, is ratified by the United States Senate.

02-07.text: The U.S. state of Mississippi officially certifies the Thirteenth Amendment, becoming the last state to approve the abolition of slavery. The Thirteenth Amendment was formally ratified by Mississippi in 1995.
The 11th Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified.
American Revolutionary War: French and Spanish forces lift the Great Siege of Gibraltar.

02-08.text: The Boy Scouts of America was incorporated, later becoming part of civic life and civil society debates.

Delaware refuses to ratify the Thirteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. Slavery was outlawed in the United States, including Delaware, when the Amendment was ratified by the requisite number of states on December 6, 1865. Delaware ratified the Thirteenth Amendment on February 12, 1901, which was the ninety-second anniversary of the birth of Abraham Lincoln.
United States President Warren G. Harding introduces the first radio set in the White House.

02-09.text: After no candidate receives a majority of electoral votes in the US presidential election of 1824, the United States House of Representatives elects John Quincy Adams as President of the United States.
US president Grover Cleveland signs a bill elevating the United States Department of Agriculture to a Cabinet-level agency.
American Civil War: Jefferson Davis is elected the Provisional President of the Confederate States of America by the Confederate convention at Montgomery, Alabama.

02-10.text: The 25th Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified.
United States President Dwight Eisenhower warns against United States intervention in Vietnam.
American Civil War: A Union naval flotilla destroys the bulk of the Confederate Mosquito Fleet in the Battle of Elizabeth City on the Pasquotank River in North Carolina.
02-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-11.text: U.S.President Dwight D. Eisenhower denies all appeals for clemency for Julius and Ethel Rosenberg.
Eighty-seven countries, including the United States, United Kingdom, and Soviet Union, sign the Seabed Arms Control Treaty outlawing nuclear weapons on the ocean floor in international waters.
02-11.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

02-11.text: American Civil War: The United States House of Representatives unanimously passes a resolution guaranteeing noninterference with slavery in any state.

02-12.text: Abraham Lincoln was born; his presidency tested executive power, federalism, and constitutional union.
United States President Bill Clinton is acquitted by the United States Senate in his impeachment trial.

African American United States Army veteran Isaac Woodard is severely beaten by a South Carolina police officer to the point where he loses his vision in both eyes. The incident later galvanizes the Civil Rights Movement and partially inspires Orson Welles' film Touch of Evil.
02-12.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

02-13.text: Died: Antonin Scalia, American lawyer and judge, Associate Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States (b. 1936)
02-13.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-13.text: Born: Robert H. Jackson, American lawyer, judge, and politician, 57th United States Attorney General (d. 1954)
02-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-13.text: Born: John Aaron Rawlins, American general and politician, 29th United States Secretary of War (d. 1869)
02-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-14.text: In New York City, James Knox Polk becomes the first serving President of the United States to have his photograph taken.
United American Bank of Knoxville, Tennessee collapses. Its president, Jake Butcher, is later convicted of fraud.
World War II: On the first day of the bombing of Dresden, the British Royal Air Force and the United States Army Air Forces begin fire-bombing Dresden.
02-14.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-15.text: Women's rights: US President Rutherford B. Hayes signs a bill allowing female attorneys to argue cases before the Supreme Court of the United States.
02-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-15.text: The battleship USS Maine explodes and sinks in Havana harbor in Cuba, killing 274. This event leads the United States to declare war on Spain.
02-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-15.text: American Civil War: General Ulysses S. Grant attacks Fort Donelson, Tennessee.
02-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-16.text: American Civil War: General Ulysses S. Grant captures Fort Donelson, Tennessee.
02-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-17.text: In Wesberry v. Sanders the Supreme Court of the United States rules that congressional districts have to be approximately equal in population.
02-17.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-17.text: An electoral tie between Thomas Jefferson and Aaron Burr is resolved when Jefferson is elected President of the United States and Burr, Vice President by the United States House of Representatives.
American Civil War: Columbia, South Carolina, is burned as Confederate forces flee from advancing Union forces.
02-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-18.text: Congress passes a law admitting the state of Vermont to the Union, effective 4 March, after that state had existed for 14 years as a de facto independent largely unrecognized state.
American Civil War: Union forces under Major General William T. Sherman set the South Carolina State House on fire during the burning of Columbia.
02-18.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-19.text: President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 9066, authorizing Japanese American internment during World War II.
Former Vice President of the United States Aaron Burr is arrested for treason in Wakefield, Alabama and confined to Fort Stoddert.
World War II: Battle of Iwo Jima: About 30,000 United States Marines land on the island of Iwo Jima.
02-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-20.text: The Saturday Evening Post publishes the first of Norman Rockwell's Four Freedoms in support of United States President Franklin Roosevelt's 1941 State of the Union address theme of Four Freedoms.
The Congress of the United States proposes the Twenty-first Amendment to the United States Constitution that will end Prohibition in the United States.
The Congress of the United States approves the construction of the San Francisco-Oakland Bay Bridge by the state of California.

02-21.text: United States President Richard Nixon visits the People's Republic of China to normalize Sino-American relations.
American Civil War: Battle of Valverde is fought near Fort Craig in New Mexico Territory.
02-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-22.text: U.S. President Calvin Coolidge becomes the first President to deliver a radio address from the White House.
Aldrich Ames and his wife are charged by the United States Department of Justice with spying for the Soviet Union.
By the Adams-Onís Treaty, Spain sells Florida to the United States for five million U.S. dollars.
02-22.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

02-23.text: U.S. President Calvin Coolidge signs a bill by Congress establishing the Federal Radio Commission (later replaced by the Federal Communications Commission) which was to regulate the use of radio frequencies in the United States.
Mexican-American War: Battle of Buena Vista: In Mexico, American troops under future president General Zachary Taylor defeat Mexican General Antonio López de Santa Anna.

World War II: During the Battle of Iwo Jima, a group of United States Marines and a U.S. Navy hospital corpsman reach the top of Mount Suribachi on the island and are photographed raising the American flag.
02-23.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-24.text: The Supreme Court decided Marbury v. Madison, establishing judicial review.
02-24.ap_connection: Judicial review; Supreme Court; checks and balances

02-24.text: Andrew Johnson becomes the first President of the United States to be impeached by the United States House of Representatives. He is later acquitted in the Senate.
A special commission of the United States Congress condemns the Japanese American internment during World War II.

02-25.text: Hiram Rhodes Revels, a Republican from Mississippi, is sworn into the United States Senate, becoming the first African American ever to sit in the U.S. Congress.
The first Pan American Games were officially opened in Buenos Aires, Argentina by President Juan Perón.

02-26.text: Died: Robert R. Livingston, American lawyer and politician, 1st United States Secretary of Foreign Affairs (b. 1746)
02-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-26.text: Born: Tim Kaine, American lawyer and politician, 70th Governor of Virginia
02-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-26.text: Born: David Beasley, American lawyer and politician, 113th Governor of South Carolina
02-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-27.text: The 22nd Amendment was ratified, limiting presidents to two elected terms.
02-27.ap_connection: Presidency; constitutional amendments; checks on power

02-27.text: United States labor law: The U.S. Supreme Court rules that sit-down strikes violate property owners' rights and are therefore illegal.
02-27.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-27.text: A challenge to the Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, allowing women the right to vote, is rebuffed by the Supreme Court of the United States in Leser v. Garnett.
02-27.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

02-28.text: Seventy years of Holy See-United States relations are ended by a Congressional ban on federal funding of diplomatic envoys to the Vatican and are not restored until January 10, 1984.
The Battle of the Sacramento River during the Mexican-American War is a decisive victory for the United States leading to the capture of Chihuahua.
02-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-28.text: A gun on USS Princeton explodes while the boat is on a Potomac River cruise, killing six people, including two United States Cabinet members.

02-29.text: American Civil War: Kilpatrick-Dahlgren Raid fails: Plans to free 15,000 Union soldiers being held near Richmond, Virginia are thwarted.
02-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

02-29.text: The Jay Treaty between the United States and Great Britain comes into force, facilitating ten years of peaceful trade between the two nations.
02-29.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-01.text: The Articles of Confederation went into effect, creating the first national government of the United States.
03-01.ap_connection: Articles of Confederation; federalism; weaknesses of national government

03-01.text: United States President John F. Kennedy establishes the Peace Corps.
United States President John Tyler signs a bill authorizing the United States to annex the Republic of Texas.

03-02.text: The U.S. Congress passes the Platt Amendment limiting the autonomy of Cuba, as a condition of the withdrawal of American troops.
World War II: Battle of the Bismarck Sea: United States and Australian forces sink Japanese convoy ships.
03-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-02.text: United States Steel Corporation is founded as a result of a merger between Carnegie Steel Company and Federal Steel Company which became the first corporation in the world with a market capital over $1 billion.
03-02.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-03.text: Censorship in the United States: The U.S. Congress enacts the Comstock Law, making it illegal to send any "obscene, lewd, or lascivious" books through the mail.
The U.S. Congress passes the Missouri Compromise.
American Revolutionary War: The Continental Army is routed at the Battle of Brier Creek near Savannah, Georgia.

03-04.text: The Constitution took effect as the framework for the new federal government.
John Adams is inaugurated as the 2nd President of the United States of America, becoming the first President to begin his presidency on March 4.

Gay rights: Oncale v. Sundowner Offshore Services, Inc.: The Supreme Court of the United States rules that federal laws banning on-the-job sexual harassment also apply when both parties are the same sex.
03-04.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

03-05.text: Boston Massacre: Five Americans, including Crispus Attucks, are fatally shot by British troops in an event that would contribute to the outbreak of the American Revolutionary War (also known as the American War of Independence) five years later.
Antonio de Ulloa, the first Spanish governor of Louisiana, arrives in New Orleans.
03-05.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-06.text: The Supreme Court decided Dred Scott v. Sandford, denying citizenship to enslaved and formerly enslaved Black people.
03-06.ap_connection: Civil rights; Supreme Court; constitutional conflict

03-07.text: Bloody Sunday: a group of 600 civil rights marchers is brutally attacked by state and local police in Selma, Alabama.
03-07.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

03-07.text: Vietnam War: The United States and South Vietnamese military begin Operation Truong Cong Dinh to root out Viet Cong forces from the area surrounding Mỹ Tho.
03-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-07.text: American Civil War: Union forces defeat Confederate troops at the Pea Ridge in northwestern Arkansas.
03-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-08.text: The 1957 Georgia Memorial to Congress, which petitions the U.S. Congress to declare the ratification of the 14th and 15th Amendments to the U.S. Constitution null and void, is adopted by the U.S. state of Georgia.
Thirty-five hundred United States Marines are the first American land combat forces committed during the Vietnam War.
03-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-08.text: The United States Senate votes to limit filibusters by adopting the cloture rule.

03-09.text: The U.S. Supreme Court rules in the United States v. The Amistad case that captive Africans who had seized control of the ship carrying them had been taken into slavery illegally.
03-09.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

03-09.text: American Civil War: The USS Monitor and CSS Virginia fight to a draw in the Battle of Hampton Roads, the first battle between two ironclad warships.
03-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-10.text: The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo is ratified by the United States Senate, ending the Mexican-American War.
Vietnam War: Battle of Lima Site 85, concluding the 11th with largest single ground combat loss of United States Air Force members (12) during that war.
03-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-10.text: Louisiana Purchase: In St. Louis, Missouri, a formal ceremony is conducted to transfer ownership of the Louisiana Territory from France to the United States.
03-10.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-11.text: World War II: United States President Franklin D. Roosevelt signs the Lend-Lease Act into law, allowing American-built war supplies to be shipped to the Allies on loan.
American Civil War: The Constitution of the Confederate States of America is adopted.
Janet Reno is confirmed by the United States Senate and sworn in the next day, becoming the first female Attorney General of the United States.

03-12.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt gave his first fireside chat, using radio to build public support for banking reforms.
American Civil War: The Red River Campaign begins as a US Navy fleet of 13 Ironclads and 7 Gunboats and other support ships enter the Red River

03-13.text: The United States Department of Justice announces that Exxon has agreed to pay $1 billion for the clean-up of the Exxon Valdez oil spill in Alaska.
American Civil War: The Confederate States of America agree to the use of African-American troops.
03-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-13.text: American Civil War: The U.S. federal government forbids all Union army officers from returning fugitive slaves, thus effectively annulling the Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 and setting the stage for the Emancipation Proclamation.
03-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-14.text: The Hay-Herrán Treaty, granting the United States the right to build the Panama Canal, is ratified by the United States Senate. The Colombian Senate would later reject the treaty.
The Gold Standard Act is ratified, placing United States currency on the gold standard.
American Revolutionary War: Spanish forces capture Fort Charlotte in Mobile, Alabama, the last British frontier post capable of threatening New Orleans in Spanish Louisiana.

03-15.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson addressed Congress after Selma and called for voting rights legislation.
United States President Woodrow Wilson sends 4,800 United States troops over the U.S.-Mexico border to pursue Pancho Villa.
American Civil War: The Red River Campaign: U.S. Navy fleet arrives at Alexandria, Louisiana.

03-16.text: Mississippi formally ratifies the Thirteenth Amendment, becoming the last state to approve the abolition of slavery. The Thirteenth Amendment was officially ratified in 1865.
American Civil War: The Battle of Averasborough began as Confederate forces suffer irreplaceable casualties in the final months of the war.
03-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-16.text: American Civil War: During the Red River Campaign, Union troops reach Alexandria, Louisiana.

03-17.text: The Pulitzer Prize-winning photograph Burst of Joy is taken, depicting a former prisoner of war being reunited with his family, which came to symbolize the end of United States involvement in the Vietnam War.
03-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-18.text: The Supreme Court decided Gideon v. Wainwright, requiring states to provide attorneys for defendants who cannot afford one in felony cases.
03-18.ap_connection: Due process; selective incorporation; rights of the accused

03-18.text: American Civil War: The Congress of the Confederate States adjourns for the last time.
The War Relocation Authority is established in the United States to take Japanese Americans into custody.
03-18.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-19.text: The United States Senate rejects the Treaty of Versailles for the second time (the first time was on November 19, 1919).
American Civil War: The Battle of Bentonville begins. By the end of the battle two days later, Confederate forces had retreated from Four Oaks, North Carolina.
03-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-20.text: The United States Senate ratifies a peace treaty with Japan.
Invasion of Iraq: In the early hours of the morning, the United States and three other countries (the UK, Australia and Poland) begin military operations in Iraq.
03-20.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-21.text: US President Jimmy Carter announces a United States boycott of the 1980 Summer Olympics in Moscow to protest the Soviet war in Afghanistan.
Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. leads 3,200 people on the start of the third and finally successful civil rights march from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama.
03-21.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action
American political development; institutions; public policy

03-22.text: Congress sent the Equal Rights Amendment to the states for ratification, though it did not become part of the Constitution.
In Eisenstadt v. Baird, the United States Supreme Court decides that unmarried persons have the right to possess contraceptives.
03-22.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

03-22.text: In North Carolina, William Woods Holden becomes the first governor of a U.S. state to be removed from office by impeachment.
03-22.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-23.text: The first of The Nixon Interviews (12 will be recorded over four weeks) are videotaped with British journalist David Frost interviewing former United States President Richard Nixon about the Watergate scandal and the Nixon tapes.
American Revolutionary War: Patrick Henry delivers his speech - "Give me liberty, or give me death!" - at St. John's Episcopal Church, Richmond, Virginia.
The First Battle of Kernstown, Virginia, marks the start of Stonewall Jackson's Valley Campaign. Although a Confederate defeat, the engagement distracts Federal efforts to capture Richmond.

03-24.text: United States Congress passes the Tydings-McDuffie Act, allowing the Philippines to become a self-governing commonwealth.

03-25.text: Civil rights activists led by Martin Luther King Jr. successfully complete their 4-day 50-mile march from Selma to the capitol in Montgomery, Alabama.
03-25.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

03-25.text: American Civil War: In Virginia, Confederate forces temporarily capture Fort Stedman from the Union.
03-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-26.text: The Supreme Court decided Baker v. Carr, opening the door to federal court review of legislative districting.

03-27.text: World War II: Battle of the Komandorski Islands: In the Aleutian Islands the battle begins when United States Navy forces intercept Japanese attempting to reinforce a garrison at Kiska.
03-27.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-27.text: The United States Government establishes a permanent navy and authorizes the building of six frigates.
03-27.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-28.text: United States President George H. W. Bush posthumously awards Jesse Owens the Congressional Gold Medal.
Cold War: The United States Department of State releases the Acheson-Lilienthal Report, outlining a plan for the international control of nuclear power.
03-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-28.text: American Civil War: In the Battle of Glorieta Pass, Union forces stop the Confederate invasion of the New Mexico Territory. The battle began on March 26.
03-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-29.text: The Twenty-third Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, allowing residents of Washington, D.C., to vote in presidential elections.
Vietnam War: The last United States combat soldiers leave South Vietnam.
03-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-29.text: American Civil War: Federal forces under Major General Philip Sheridan move to flank Confederate forces under Robert E. Lee as the Appomattox Campaign begins.

03-30.text: Origins of the American Civil War: "Border Ruffians" from Missouri invade Kansas and force election of a pro-slavery legislature.
Vietnam War: A car bomb explodes in front of the United States Embassy, Saigon, killing 22 and wounding 183 others.
03-30.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

03-30.text: The Florida Territory is created in the United States.
03-30.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-31.text: The United States takes possession of the Danish West Indies after paying $25 million to Denmark, and renames the territory the United States Virgin Islands.
03-31.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

03-31.text: American Revolutionary War: The Kingdom of Great Britain orders the port of Boston, Massachusetts closed pursuant to the Boston Port Act.

04-01.text: President Richard Nixon signs the Public Health Cigarette Smoking Act into law, requiring the Surgeon General's warnings on tobacco products and banning cigarette advertising on television and radio in the United States, effective 1 January 1971.
United States President Dwight D. Eisenhower authorizes the creation of the United States Air Force Academy in Colorado.
World War II: The Tenth United States Army attacks the Thirty-Second Japanese Army on Okinawa.
04-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-02.text: President Woodrow Wilson asked Congress for a declaration of war against Germany.
United States President Jimmy Carter signs the Crude Oil Windfall Profits Tax Act.
The United States Congress passes the Foraker Act, giving Puerto Rico limited self-rule.

04-03.text: United States President Harry S. Truman signs the Marshall Plan, authorizing $5 billion in aid for 16 countries.
United States v. Microsoft Corp.: Microsoft is ruled to have violated United States antitrust law by keeping "an oppressive thumb" on its competitors.
04-03.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

04-03.text: Vietnam War: United States Secretary of Defense Melvin Laird announces that the United States will start to "Vietnamize" the war effort.
04-03.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-04.text: Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated, intensifying national debate over civil rights, protest, and federal action.
04-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; social movements; public policy

04-04.text: American Civil War: A day after Union forces capture Richmond, Virginia, U.S. President Abraham Lincoln visits the Confederate capital.

William Henry Harrison dies of pneumonia, becoming the first President of the United States to die in office, and setting the record for the briefest administration. Vice President John Tyler succeeds Harrison as President.

04-05.text: United States President George Washington exercises his authority to veto a bill, the first time this power is used in the United States.
American Civil War: The Battle of Yorktown begins.
04-05.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-06.text: World War I: The United States declares war on Germany (see President Woodrow Wilson's address to Congress).
Huey P. Long, Governor of Louisiana, is impeached by the Louisiana House of Representatives.
The Grand Army of the Republic, an American patriotic organization composed of Union veterans of the American Civil War, is founded. It lasts until 1956.
04-06.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-07.text: Prohibition in the United States is repealed for beer of no more than 3.2% alcohol by weight, eight months before the ratification of the XXI amendment. (Now celebrated as National Beer Day in the United States of America)
United States President Dwight D. Eisenhower gives his "domino theory" speech during a news conference.
A bulldozer kills Rev. Bruce W. Klunder, a civil rights activist, during a school segregation protest in Cleveland, Ohio, sparking a riot.
04-07.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

04-08.text: The 17th Amendment to the United States Constitution, requiring direct election of Senators, becomes law.
In Pollock v. Farmers' Loan & Trust Co. the Supreme Court of the United States declares unapportioned income tax to be unconstitutional.
04-08.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

04-08.text: American Civil War: Battle of Mansfield: Union forces are thwarted by the Confederate army at Mansfield, Louisiana.
04-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-09.text: Congress passed the Civil Rights Act of 1866 over President Andrew Johnson's veto.

The Journey of Reconciliation, the first interracial Freedom Ride begins through the upper South in violation of Jim Crow laws. The riders wanted enforcement of the United States Supreme Court's 1946 Irene Morgan decision that banned racial segregation in interstate travel.
04-09.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

04-09.text: Marian Anderson sings at the Lincoln Memorial, after being denied the right to sing at the Daughters of the American Revolution's Constitution Hall.

04-10.text: American Civil War: A day after his surrender to Union forces, Confederate General Robert E. Lee addresses his troops for the last time.
04-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-10.text: The Federal government of the United States approves the creation of the Second Bank of the United States.
04-10.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

04-11.text: FBI Miami Shootout: A gun battle in broad daylight in Dade County, Florida between two bank/armored car robbers and pursuing FBI agents. During the firefight, FBI agents Jerry L. Dove and Benjamin P. Grogan were killed, while five other agents were wounded. As a result, the popular .40 S&W cartridge was developed.
04-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-12.text: United States President Bill Clinton is cited for contempt of court for giving "intentionally false statements" in a civil lawsuit; he is later fined and disbarred.
American Revolution: With the Halifax Resolves, the North Carolina Provincial Congress authorizes its Congressional delegation to vote for independence from Britain.
American Civil War: Mobile, Alabama, falls to the Union Army.
04-12.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-13.text: Thomas Jefferson was born; his ideas shaped the Declaration of Independence and debates over limited government.
04-13.ap_connection: Declaration of Independence; natural rights; limited government

04-13.text: The United States Treasury Department reintroduces the two-dollar bill as a Federal Reserve Note on Thomas Jefferson's 233rd birthday as part of the United States Bicentennial celebration.
American Civil War: Raleigh, North Carolina is occupied by Union Forces.
04-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-14.text: Born: David G. Burnet, American politician, 2nd Vice-President of Texas (d. 1870)
Born: Arnold Burns, American lawyer and politician, 21st United States Deputy Attorney General (d. 2013)
04-14.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-14.text: Born: David Justice, American baseball player and sportscaster
04-14.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

04-15.text: President Abraham Lincoln calls for 75,000 Volunteers to quell the insurrection that soon became the American Civil War.
Preliminary articles of peace ending the American Revolutionary War (or American War of Independence) are ratified.

At Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina, Ella Baker leads a conference that results in the creation of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, one of the principal organizations of the Civil Rights Movement in the 1960s.
04-15.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

04-16.text: The United States Senate ratifies the Rush-Bagot Treaty, establishing the border with Canada.
Bernard Baruch first applies the term "Cold War" to describe the relationship between the United States and the Soviet Union.
04-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-16.text: The United States Army liberates Nazi Sonderlager (high security) prisoner-of-war camp Oflag IV-C (better known as Colditz).
04-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-17.text: The Supreme Court decided Lochner v. New York, limiting some economic regulations under liberty-of-contract reasoning.
04-17.ap_connection: Judicial review; economic liberty; due process

04-17.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Plymouth begins: Confederate forces attack Plymouth, North Carolina.
04-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-17.text: American Civil War: Grierson's Raid begins: Troops under Union Army Colonel Benjamin Grierson attack central Mississippi.
04-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-18.text: The United States launches Operation Praying Mantis against Iranian naval forces in the largest naval battle since World War II.
04-18.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-19.text: The Battles of Lexington and Concord began armed conflict between colonists and British authority.
04-19.ap_connection: Revolutionary origins; consent of the governed

04-19.text: American Civil War: Baltimore riot of 1861: A pro-Secession mob in Baltimore attacks United States Army troops marching through the city.
04-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-19.text: John Adams secures the Dutch Republic's recognition of the United States as an independent government. The house which he had purchased in The Hague, Netherlands becomes the first American embassy.
04-19.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

04-20.text: President William McKinley signed a joint resolution to Congress for declaration of War against Spain, beginning the Spanish-American War.
U.S. Congress passes an act creating the Wisconsin Territory.
American Civil War: Robert E. Lee resigns his commission in the United States Army in order to command the forces of the state of Virginia.
04-20.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-21.text: Spanish-American War: The United States Navy begins a blockade of Cuban ports. When the U.S. Congress issued a declaration of war on April 25, it declared that a state of war had existed from this date.
The Seattle World's Fair (Century 21 Exposition) opens. It is the first World's Fair in the United States since World War II.
04-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-21.text: The compound of the militant group The Covenant, The Sword, and the Arm of the Lord surrenders to federal authorities in Arkansas after a two-day government siege.
04-21.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

04-22.text: The U.S. Congress passes the Coinage Act of 1864 that mandates that the inscription In God We Trust be placed on all coins minted as United States currency.
The United Kingdom, Japan and the United States sign the London Naval Treaty regulating submarine warfare and limiting shipbuilding.
04-22.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-23.text: American journalist William N. Oatis is arrested for espionage by the Communist government of Czechoslovakia.
04-23.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

04-24.text: The United States Library of Congress is established when President John Adams signs legislation to appropriate $5,000 to purchase "such books as may be necessary for the use of Congress".
In the United States, the Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996 is passed into law.
04-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-24.text: Vietnam War: American General William Westmoreland says in a news conference that the enemy had "gained support in the United States that gives him hope that he can win politically that which he cannot win militarily."
04-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-25.text: Spanish-American War: The United States declares war on Spain.
04-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-25.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Marks' Mills.
04-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-25.text: American Civil War: Forces under U.S. Admiral David Farragut demand the surrender of the Confederate city of New Orleans, Louisiana.
04-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-26.text: World War II: Filipino troops of the 66th Infantry Regiment, Philippine Commonwealth Army, USAFIP-NL and the American troops of the 33rd and 37th Infantry Division, United States Army are liberated in Baguio City and they fight against the Japanese forces under General Tomoyuki Yamashita.
04-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-26.text: American Civil War: Confederate General Joseph E. Johnston surrenders his army to General William Tecumseh Sherman at the Bennett Place near Durham, North Carolina. Also the date of Confederate Memorial Day for two states.
04-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-26.text: First Barbary War: United States Marines captured Derne under the command of First Lieutenant Presley O'Bannon.
04-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-27.text: Former United States President Nixon aide John D. Ehrlichman is released from an Arizona prison after serving 18 months for Watergate-related crimes.
Following the resignation and death of William P. Frye, a compromise is reached to rotate the office of President pro tempore of the United States Senate.
American President Abraham Lincoln suspends the writ of habeas corpus.

04-28.text: Maryland becomes the seventh state to ratify the United States Constitution.
Occupied Japan by the Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers: The United States occupation of Japan ends as the Treaty of San Francisco, ratified September 8, 1951, comes into force.
Vietnam War: U.S. President Richard Nixon formally authorizes American combat troops to fight communist sanctuaries in Cambodia.

04-29.text: Watergate scandal: United States President Richard Nixon announces the release of edited transcripts of White House tape recordings relating to the scandal.
Vietnam War: United States and South Vietnamese forces invade Cambodia to hunt Viet Cong.
04-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-29.text: Theta Xi fraternity is founded at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, the only fraternity to be founded during the American Civil War.
04-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

04-30.text: George Washington took the first presidential oath of office in New York City.
Watergate scandal: U.S. President Richard Nixon announces that White House Counsel John Dean has been fired and that other top aides, most notably H. R. Haldeman and John Ehrlichman, have resigned.
Former Vice President and Democratic Senator Alben Barkley dies during a speech in Virginia.

05-01.text: The Memphis Race Riots begin. In three days time, 46 blacks and two whites were killed. Reports of the atrocities influenced passage of the Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution.
Spanish-American War: Battle of Manila Bay: The United States Navy destroys the Spanish Pacific fleet in the first major battle of the war.
05-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-01.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Chancellorsville begins.
05-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-02.text: Osama bin Laden, the suspected mastermind behind the September 11 attacks and the FBI's most wanted man, is killed by the United States special forces in Abbottabad, Pakistan.
05-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-02.text: President Bill Clinton announces that accurate GPS access would no longer be restricted to the United States military.
American Civil War: Stonewall Jackson is wounded by friendly fire while returning to camp after reconnoitering during the Battle of Chancellorsville. He succumbs to pneumonia eight days later.
05-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-03.text: The United States Senate Committee on Armed Services and United States Senate Committee on Foreign Relations begin their closed door hearings into the dismissal of General Douglas MacArthur by U.S. President Harry Truman.

The police force in Birmingham, Alabama switches tactics and responds with violent force to stop the "Birmingham campaign" protesters. Images of the violent suppression are transmitted worldwide, bringing new-found attention to the Civil Rights Movement.
05-03.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-03.text: The first unsolicited bulk commercial email (which would later become known as "spam") is sent by a Digital Equipment Corporation marketing representative to every ARPANET address on the west coast of the United States.
05-03.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-04.text: The Freedom Rides began, challenging segregation in interstate bus travel.
05-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; interstate commerce; federal enforcement

05-04.text: American civil rights movement: The "Freedom Riders" begin a bus trip through the South.
05-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-04.text: Vietnam War: Kent State shootings: The Ohio National Guard, sent to Kent State University after disturbances in the city of Kent the weekend before, opens fire killing four unarmed students and wounding nine others. The students were protesting the Cambodian Campaign of the United States and South Vietnam.

05-05.text: Iran-Contra affair: Start of Congressional televised hearings in the United States of America
American Civil War: The Confederate District of the Gulf surrenders about 4,000 men at Citronelle, Alabama.
05-05.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-05.text: American Civil War: The Battle of the Wilderness begins in Spotsylvania County.
05-05.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-06.text: The United States Congress passes the Chinese Exclusion Act.
American Civil War: The Battle of Chancellorsville ends with the defeat of the Army of the Potomac by Confederate troops.
05-06.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-06.text: American Civil War: Arkansas secedes from the Union.
05-06.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-07.text: Michigan ratifies a 203-year-old proposed amendment to the United States Constitution making the 27th Amendment law. This amendment bars the U.S. Congress from giving itself a mid-term pay raise.

World War I: German submarine U-20 sinks RMS Lusitania, killing 1,198 people, including 128 Americans. Public reaction to the sinking turns many formerly pro-Germans in the United States against the German Empire
05-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-07.text: American Civil War: The Army of the Potomac, under General Ulysses S. Grant, breaks off from the Battle of the Wilderness and moves southwards.
05-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-08.text: World War II: The Battle of the Coral Sea comes to an end with Imperial Japanese Navy aircraft carrier aircraft attacking and sinking the United States Navy aircraft carrier USS Lexington. The battle marks the first time in the naval history that two enemy fleets fight without visual contact between warring ships.
05-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-08.text: American Civil War: Richmond, Virginia is named the capital of the Confederate States of America.
05-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-08.text: A 71-day standoff between federal authorities and the American Indian Movement members occupying the Pine Ridge Reservation at Wounded Knee, South Dakota ends with the surrender of the militants.
05-08.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

05-09.text: Watergate scandal: The United States House Committee on the Judiciary opens formal and public impeachment hearings against President Richard Nixon.
American Civil War: President Andrew Johnson issues a proclamation ending belligerent rights of the rebels and enjoining foreign nations to intern or expel Confederate ships.
Vietnam War: In Washington, D.C., 75,000 to 100,000 war protesters demonstrate in front of the White House.

05-10.text: Victoria Woodhull becomes the first woman nominated for President of the United States.
American Revolutionary War: Representatives from the Thirteen Colonies begin the Second Continental Congress in Philadelphia.
FBI agent Robert Hanssen is sentenced to life imprisonment without the possibility of parole for selling United States secrets to Russia for $1.4 million in cash and diamonds.
05-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-11.text: Citing government misconduct, Daniel Ellsberg's charges for his involvement in releasing the Pentagon Papers to The New York Times are dismissed.
05-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-11.text: An act of the U.S. Congress establishes Glacier National Park in Montana.
President James K. Polk asked for a Declaration of War against Mexico, starting the Mexican-American War. It is approved on May 13.

05-12.text: Former US President Jimmy Carter arrives in Cuba for a five-day visit with Fidel Castro, becoming the first President of the United States, in or out of office, to visit the island since Castro's 1959 revolution.
American Civil War: The Battle of Palmito Ranch: The first day of the last major land action to take place during the Civil War, resulting in a Confederate victory.
05-12.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-12.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Spotsylvania Court House: Thousands of Union and Confederate soldiers die in "the Bloody Angle".
05-12.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-13.text: An F3 tornado hits Kalamazoo County, Michigan. President Jimmy Carter declares it a federal disaster area.
During a visit to Caracas, Venezuela, Vice President Richard Nixon's car is attacked by anti-American demonstrators.
American Civil War: Battle of Palmito Ranch: In far south Texas, the last land battle of the Civil War ends with a Confederate victory.
05-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-14.text: In Philadelphia, delegates convene a Constitutional Convention to write a new Constitution for the United States; George Washington presides.
Civil Rights Movement: The Freedom Riders bus is fire-bombed near Anniston, Alabama, and the civil rights protesters are beaten by an angry mob.
05-14.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-14.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Jackson takes place.
05-14.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-15.text: California becomes the second U.S. state after Massachusetts in 2004 to legalize same-sex marriage after the state's own Supreme Court rules a previous ban unconstitutional.
05-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-15.text: President Richard Nixon appoints Anna Mae Hays and Elizabeth P. Hoisington the first female United States Army generals.

In Standard Oil Co. of New Jersey v. United States, the United States Supreme Court declares Standard Oil to be an "unreasonable" monopoly under the Sherman Antitrust Act and orders the company to be broken up.
05-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-16.text: United States President Andrew Johnson is acquitted in his impeachment trial by one vote in the United States Senate.
Queen Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom addresses a joint session of the United States Congress. She is the first British monarch to address the U.S. Congress.
The United States Congress establishes the nickel.

05-17.text: The Supreme Court decided Brown v. Board of Education, ruling school segregation unconstitutional.
05-17.ap_connection: Equal protection; civil rights; Supreme Court

05-17.text: Watergate scandal: Televised hearings begin in the United States Senate.

05-18.text: The Supreme Court decided Plessy v. Ferguson, approving 'separate but equal' segregation.
World War I: The Selective Service Act of 1917 is passed, giving the President of the United States the power of conscription.
Abraham Lincoln wins the Republican Party presidential nomination over William H. Seward, who later becomes the United States Secretary of State.

05-19.text: The United States Congress passes the Emergency Quota Act establishing national quotas on immigration.
U.S. President John Quincy Adams signs the Tariff of 1828 into law, protecting wool manufacturers in the United States.

Mexican-American War: Mexico ratifies the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo thus ending the war and ceding California, Nevada, Utah and parts of four other modern-day U.S. states to the United States for US$15 million.

05-20.text: Civil rights: The Supreme Court of the United States rules in Romer v. Evans against a law that would have prevented any city, town or county in the state of Colorado from taking any legislative, executive, or judicial action to protect the rights of gays and lesbians.
05-20.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-20.text: Cuba gains independence from the United States. Tomás Estrada Palma becomes the country's first President.
In the United States, the Armed Forces Security Agency, the predecessor to the National Security Agency, is established.
05-20.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-21.text: American civil rights movement: Alabama Governor John Malcolm Patterson declares martial law in an attempt to restore order after race riots break out.
05-21.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-21.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Spotsylvania Court House ends.
05-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-21.text: American Civil War: The Union Army succeeds in closing off the last escape route from Port Hudson, Louisiana, in preparation for the coming siege.
05-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-22.text: Congressman Preston Brooks of South Carolina severely beats Senator Charles Sumner of Massachusetts with a cane in the hall of the United States Senate for a speech Sumner had made regarding Southerners and slavery.
A grand jury indicts former Vice President of the United States Aaron Burr on a charge of treason.
Civil Rights Movement: A jury in Birmingham, Alabama, convicts former Ku Klux Klan member Bobby Frank Cherry of the 1963 murders of four girls in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing.
05-22.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-23.text: South Carolina ratifies the United States Constitution as the eighth American state.
Mexican-American War: President Mariano Paredes of Mexico unofficially declares war on the United States.
American Civil War: Sergeant William Harvey Carney is awarded the Medal of Honor for his heroism in the Assault on the Battery Wagner in 1863.
05-23.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-24.text: Samuel Morse sends the message "What hath God wrought" (a biblical quotation, Numbers 23:23) from the Old Supreme Court Chamber in the United States Capitol to his assistant, Alfred Vail, in Baltimore, Maryland, to inaugurate a commercial telegraph line between Baltimore and Washington D.C.
05-24.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-24.text: American civil rights movement: Freedom Riders are arrested in Jackson, Mississippi, for "disturbing the peace" after disembarking from their bus.
05-24.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

05-24.text: Russia and the United States sign the Moscow Treaty.
05-24.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

05-25.text: The Constitutional Convention opened in Philadelphia.
A treaty between Pennsylvania and Maryland ends the Conojocular War with settlement of a boundary dispute and exchange of prisoners.
05-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-26.text: The Supreme Court of the United States rules that Ellis Island, the historic gateway for millions of immigrants, is mainly in the state of New Jersey, not New York.
05-26.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-26.text: The U.S. Congress passes Public Law 80-557, which permanently establishes the Civil Air Patrol as an auxiliary of the United States Air Force.
The United States and the Soviet Union sign the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty.
05-26.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

05-27.text: Barack Obama is the first president of United States to visit Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park and meet Hibakusha.
New Deal: The Supreme Court of the United States declares the National Industrial Recovery Act to be unconstitutional in A.L.A. Schechter Poultry Corp. v. United States, (295 U.S. 495).
05-27.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

05-27.text: American Civil War: First Assault on the Confederate works at the Siege of Port Hudson.
05-27.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-28.text: U.S. President Bill Clinton's former business partners in the Whitewater land deal, Jim McDougal and Susan McDougal, and the Governor of Arkansas Jim Guy Tucker, are convicted of fraud.

05-29.text: Rhode Island becomes the last of the original United States' colonies to ratify the Constitution and is admitted as the 13th U.S. state.
Michele Schirru, a citizen of the United States, is executed by Italian military firing squad for intent to kill Benito Mussolini.
05-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-29.text: American Revolutionary War: At the Battle of Waxhaws, the British continue attacking after the Continentals lay down their arms, killing 113 and critically wounding all but 53 that remained.

05-30.text: Died: Beau Biden, American soldier, lawyer, and politician, 44th Attorney General of Delaware (b. 1969)
05-30.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

05-31.text: The United States Senate votes to cut off funding for the bombing of Khmer Rouge targets within Cambodia, hastening the end of the Cambodian Civil War.
American Civil War: Overland Campaign: Battle of Cold Harbor: The Army of Northern Virginia under Robert E. Lee engages the Army of the Potomac under Ulysses S. Grant and George Meade.

American Civil War: Peninsula Campaign: Battle of Seven Pines, also known as the "Battle of Fair Oaks": Confederate forces under Joseph E. Johnston and G.W. Smith engage Union forces under George B. McClellan outside Richmond, Virginia.

06-01.text: Louis Brandeis becomes the first Jew appointed to the United States Supreme Court.
06-01.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-01.text: American Civil War: Peninsula Campaign: The Battle of Seven Pines (or the Battle of Fair Oaks) ends inconclusively, with both sides claiming victory.
American Civil War: The Battle of Fairfax Court House is fought.
06-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-02.text: The Indian Citizenship Act granted U.S. citizenship to Native Americans born in the United States.

In Denver, Timothy McVeigh is convicted on 15 counts of murder and conspiracy for his role in the 1995 bombing of the Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in Oklahoma City, in which 168 people died. He was executed four years later.
06-02.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

06-03.text: The National Defense Act is signed into law, increasing the size of the United States National Guard by 450,000 men.
06-03.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

06-03.text: American Civil War: Battle of Cold Harbor: Union forces attack Confederate troops in Hanover County, Virginia.
06-03.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-03.text: American Civil War: Battle of Philippi (also called the Philippi Races): Union forces rout Confederate troops in Barbour County, Virginia, now West Virginia.
06-03.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-04.text: Women's rights: The U.S. Congress approves the 19th Amendment to the United States Constitution, which guarantees suffrage to women, and sends it to the U.S. states for ratification.
06-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-04.text: Jonathan Pollard pleads guilty to espionage for selling top secret United States military intelligence to Israel.
06-04.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-04.text: World War II: A hunter-killer group of the United States Navy captures the German submarine U-505: The first time a U.S. Navy vessel had captured an enemy vessel at sea since the 19th century.
06-04.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-05.text: The U.S. Congress abrogates the United States' use of the gold standard by enacting a joint resolution (48 Stat. 112) nullifying the right of creditors to demand payment in gold.
Louis Brandeis is sworn in as a Justice of the United States Supreme Court; he is the first American Jew to hold such a position.
06-05.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-05.text: Marshall Plan: In a speech at Harvard University, the United States Secretary of State George Marshall calls for economic aid to war-torn Europe.
06-05.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-06.text: In Gonzales v. Raich, the United States Supreme Court upholds a federal law banning cannabis, including medical marijuana.
06-06.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-06.text: Assassination of Robert F. Kennedy: Robert F. Kennedy, Democratic Party senator from New York and brother of 35th President John F. Kennedy, dies from gunshot wounds inflicted on June 5.
American Civil War: Battle of Memphis: Union forces capture Memphis, Tennessee, from the Confederates.
06-06.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-07.text: The United States Supreme Court overturns the conviction of Paul Cohen for disturbing the peace, setting the precedent that vulgar writing is protected under the First Amendment to the United States Constitution.
06-07.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-07.text: The Supreme Court of the United States hands down its decision in Griswold v. Connecticut, prohibiting the states from criminalizing the use of contraception by married couples.
06-07.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-07.text: Richard Henry Lee presents the "Lee Resolution" to the Continental Congress. The motion is seconded by John Adams and will lead to the United States Declaration of Independence.

06-08.text: James Madison introduced proposed amendments that would become the Bill of Rights.
06-08.ap_connection: Bill of Rights; civil liberties; Federalist/Anti-Federalist debate

06-08.text: The United States Supreme Court rules that restaurants in Washington, D.C. cannot refuse to serve black patrons.
06-08.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-08.text: American Civil War: Battle of Cross Keys: Confederate forces under General Stonewall Jackson save the Army of Northern Virginia from a Union assault on the James Peninsula led by General George B. McClellan.
06-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-09.text: McCarthyism: Joseph Welch, special counsel for the United States Army, lashes out at Senator Joseph McCarthy during hearings on whether Communism has infiltrated the Army giving McCarthy the famous rebuke, "You've done enough. Have you no sense of decency, sir, at long last? Have you left no sense of decency?"
06-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-09.text: American Civil War: Battle of Brandy Station, Virginia.
06-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-09.text: American Civil War: Stonewall Jackson concludes his successful Shenandoah Valley Campaign with a victory in the Battle of Port Republic; his tactics during the campaign are now studied by militaries around the world.

06-10.text: United States Senate breaks a 75-day filibuster against the Civil Rights Act of 1964, leading to the bill's passage.
06-10.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-10.text: American Civil War: Battle of Brice's Crossroads: Confederate troops under Nathan Bedford Forrest defeat a much larger Union force led by General Samuel D. Sturgis in Mississippi.
06-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-10.text: American Civil War: Battle of Big Bethel: Confederate troops under John B. Magruder defeat a much larger Union force led by General Ebenezer W. Pierce in Virginia.
06-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-11.text: Antonio Meucci is acknowledged as the first inventor of the telephone by the United States Congress.
The Continental Congress appoints Thomas Jefferson, John Adams, Benjamin Franklin, Roger Sherman, and Robert R. Livingston to the Committee of Five to draft a declaration of independence.

American Civil Rights Movement: Governor of Alabama George Wallace defiantly stands at the door of Foster Auditorium at the University of Alabama in an attempt to block two black students, Vivian Malone and James Hood, from attending that school.
