Later in the day, accompanied by federalized National Guard troops, they are able to register.
06-11.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-12.text: The United States Supreme Court in Loving v. Virginia declares all U.S. state laws which prohibit interracial marriage to be unconstitutional.
06-12.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-12.text: NAACP field secretary Medgar Evers is murdered in front of his home in Jackson, Mississippi by Ku Klux Klan member Byron De La Beckwith during the Civil Rights Movement.
06-12.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-12.text: American Civil War, Overland Campaign: Battle of Cold Harbor: Ulysses S. Grant gives the Confederate forces under Robert E. Lee a victory when he pulls his Union troops from their position at Cold Harbor, Virginia and moves south.

06-13.text: The Supreme Court decided Miranda v. Arizona, requiring warnings before custodial interrogation.
06-13.ap_connection: Rights of the accused; due process; selective incorporation

06-13.text: American Revolutionary War: Gilbert du Motier, Marquis de Lafayette lands near Charleston, South Carolina, in order to help the Continental Congress to train its army.
Vietnam War: The New York Times begins publication of the Pentagon Papers.
06-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-14.text: U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower signs a bill into law that places the words "under God" into the United States Pledge of Allegiance.
The Stars and Stripes is adopted by Congress as the Flag of the United States.
American Revolutionary War: the Continental Army is established by the Continental Congress, marking the birth of the United States Army.

06-15.text: The 12th Amendment was ratified, changing the Electoral College process for president and vice president.
New Hampshire approves the Twelfth Amendment to the United States Constitution, ratifying the document.

The United States Supreme Court rules in United States v. Álvarez-Machaín that it is permissible for the United States to forcibly extradite suspects in foreign countries and bring them to the United States for trial, without approval from those other countries.
06-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-16.text: A treaty annexing the Republic of Hawaii to the United States is signed; the Republic would not be dissolved until a year later.
06-16.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

06-17.text: The United States Supreme Court rules 8-1 in Abington School District v. Schempp against requiring the reciting of Bible verses and the Lord's Prayer in public schools.
06-17.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-17.text: Bonus Army: Around a thousand World War I veterans amass at the United States Capitol as the U.S. Senate considers a bill that would give them certain benefits.
American Civil War: Battle of Aldie in the Gettysburg Campaign.

06-18.text: The United States declaration of war upon the United Kingdom is signed by President James Madison.
Vietnam War: The United States uses B-52 bombers to attack National Liberation Front guerrilla fighters in South Vietnam.
06-18.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-18.text: American Revolutionary War: British troops abandon Philadelphia.

06-19.text: Union troops announced emancipation in Galveston, Texas, an event later commemorated as Juneteenth.
The Civil Rights Act of 1964 is approved after surviving an 83-day filibuster in the United States Senate.
06-19.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-19.text: The U.S. Congress prohibits slavery in United States territories, nullifying Dred Scott v. Sandford.

06-20.text: The U.S. Congress adopts the Great Seal of the United States.
American Civil War: West Virginia is admitted as the 35th U.S. state.
06-20.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-20.text: Oliver Ellsworth moves at the Federal Convention to call the government the 'United States'.
06-20.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

06-21.text: New Hampshire ratifies the Constitution of the United States and is admitted as the ninth state in the United States.
The U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Texas v. Johnson that American flag-burning was a form of political protest protected by the First Amendment.
06-21.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-21.text: In handing down the decision in Miller v. California 413 US 15, the Supreme Court of the United States establishes the Miller test for obscenity in U.S. law.
06-21.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-22.text: The United States Department of Justice is created by the U.S. Congress.

06-23.text: Title IX was enacted, banning sex discrimination in federally funded education programs.
06-23.ap_connection: Civil rights; federal policy; equal protection

06-23.text: Watergate scandal: U.S. President Richard M. Nixon and White House Chief of Staff H. R. Haldeman are taped talking about using the Central Intelligence Agency to obstruct the Federal Bureau of Investigation's investigation into the Watergate break-ins.
Title IX of the United States Civil Rights Act of 1964 is amended to prohibit sexual discrimination to any educational program receiving federal funds.
06-23.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

06-24.text: In Roth v. United States, the U.S. Supreme Court rules that obscenity is not protected by the First Amendment.
06-24.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-24.text: American Revolutionary War: The Great Siege of Gibraltar begins.

06-25.text: The Supreme Court decided Engel v. Vitale, ruling school-sponsored prayer unconstitutional.
06-25.ap_connection: Establishment Clause; civil liberties; selective incorporation

06-25.text: Virginia becomes the tenth state to ratify the United States Constitution.
In Clinton v. City of New York, the United States Supreme Court decides that the Line Item Veto Act of 1996 is unconstitutional.
06-25.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-26.text: The Supreme Court decided Obergefell v. Hodges, requiring states to license and recognize same-sex marriages.
06-26.ap_connection: Equal protection; due process; civil rights

06-26.text: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled, 5-4, that same-sex couples have a constitutional right to marriage under the 14th Amendment to the United States Constitution.
06-26.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-26.text: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled, 5-4, that Section 3 of the Defense of Marriage Act is unconstitutional and in violation of the Fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution.
06-26.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-27.text: The United States decides to send troops to fight in the Korean War.
06-27.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-27.text: Confederate forces defeat Union forces during the Battle of Kennesaw Mountain during the Atlanta Campaign of the American Civil War.

06-28.text: The United States Supreme Court, in Regents of the University of California v. Bakke bars quota systems in college admissions.
06-28.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-28.text: The United States Court of Private Land Claims rules James Reavis' claim to Barony of Arizona is "wholly fictitious and fraudulent."
06-28.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

06-28.text: The Battle of Sullivan's Island ends with the American victory in the American Revolutionary War leading to the commemoration of Carolina Day.

06-29.text: The United States Supreme Court rules in the case Furman v. Georgia that arbitrary and inconsistent imposition of the death penalty violates the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendments, and constitutes cruel and unusual punishment.
06-29.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

06-29.text: The Federal Aid Highway Act of 1956 is signed, officially creating the United States Interstate Highway System.
06-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

06-29.text: Hyde Park and several other Illinois townships vote to be annexed by Chicago, forming the largest United States city in area and second largest in population at the time.

06-30.text: U.S. President Warren G. Harding appoints former President William Howard Taft Chief Justice of the United States.
The United States Congress passes the Meat Inspection Act and Pure Food and Drug Act.
The U.S. Congress organizes the Michigan Territory.

07-01.text: The United States Department of Justice formally comes into existence.
American Civil War: The Battle of Gettysburg begins.
07-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-01.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Malvern Hill takes place. It is the last of the Seven Days Battles, part of George B. McClellan's Peninsula Campaign.

07-02.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964.
World War I: U.S. President Warren G. Harding signs the Knox-Porter Resolution formally ending the war between the United States and Imperial Germany.
The Continental Congress adopts a resolution severing ties with the Kingdom of Great Britain although the wording of the formal Declaration of Independence is not published until July 4.

07-03.text: The Constitution of Puerto Rico is approved by the United States Congress.
United States President Franklin D. Roosevelt dedicates the Eternal Light Peace Memorial and lights the eternal flame at Gettysburg Battlefield.
Congress establishes the United States' 2nd mint in San Francisco.

07-04.text: The Second Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence.
07-04.ap_connection: Natural rights; popular sovereignty; founding ideals

07-04.text: U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson signs the Freedom of Information Act into United States law. The act went into effect the next year.
President Woodrow Wilson addresses American Civil War veterans at the Great Reunion of 1913.

07-05.text: The National Labor Relations Act, which governs labor relations in the United States, is signed into law by President Franklin D. Roosevelt.
Right to vote: The Twenty-sixth Amendment to the United States Constitution, lowering the voting age from 21 to 18 years, is formally certified by President Richard Nixon.
The Second Continental Congress adopts the Olive Branch Petition.

07-06.text: An early Republican Party convention met in Jackson, Michigan, reflecting growing conflict over slavery and the future of national politics.
David Kalākaua, monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaii, is forced to sign the Bayonet Constitution, which transfers much of the king's authority to the Legislature of the Kingdom of Hawaii.
Battle of Grenada: The French defeat British naval forces during the American Revolutionary War.

07-07.text: U.S. President Ronald Reagan appoints Sandra Day O'Connor to become the first female member of the Supreme Court of the United States.
07-07.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-07.text: U.S. President William McKinley signs the Newlands Resolution annexing Hawaii as a territory of the United States.
Buddhist crisis: The police of Ngô Đình Nhu, brother and chief political adviser of President Ngô Đình Diệm, attacked a group of American journalists who were covering a protest.

07-08.text: The Olive Branch Petition is signed by the Continental Congress of the Thirteen Colonies of North America.
Church bells (possibly including the Liberty Bell) are rung after John Nixon delivers the first public reading of the Declaration of Independence of the United States.

07-09.text: The 14th Amendment was ratified, defining citizenship and protecting due process and equal protection.
07-09.ap_connection: Civil rights; due process; equal protection

07-09.text: American forces take Saipan, bringing the Japanese archipelago within range of B-29 raids, and causing the downfall of the Tojo government.
07-09.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

07-10.text: U.S. President Andrew Jackson vetoes a bill that would re-charter the Second Bank of the United States.
The United States takes possession of its newly bought territory of Florida from Spain.
07-10.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

07-11.text: Former President of the United States William Howard Taft is sworn in as 10th Chief Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court, becoming the only person ever to hold both offices.
07-11.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-11.text: A duel occurs in which the Vice President of the United States Aaron Burr mortally wounds former Secretary of the Treasury Alexander Hamilton.
American Civil War: Battle of Fort Stevens; Confederate forces attempt to invade Washington, D.C.
07-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-12.text: The Medal of Honor is authorized by the United States Congress.

07-13.text: The Continental Congress enacts the Northwest Ordinance establishing governing rules for the Northwest Territory. It also establishes procedures for the admission of new states and limits the expansion of slavery.

07-14.text: Harry Atwood, an exhibition pilot for the Wright brothers, lands his airplane at the South Lawn of the White House. He is later awarded a Gold medal from U.S. President William Howard Taft for this feat.
The Sedition Act becomes law in the United States making it a federal crime to write, publish, or utter false or malicious statements about the United States government.
07-14.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

07-15.text: Vietnam War: The United States and South Vietnam begin Operation Hastings to push the North Vietnamese out of the Vietnamese Demilitarized Zone.
07-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-15.text: Reconstruction Era of the United States: Georgia becomes the last of the former Confederate states to be readmitted to the Union.
07-15.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

07-16.text: Congress approved the Residence Act, establishing the future permanent capital on the Potomac River.

American Civil War: At the order of President Abraham Lincoln, Union troops begin a 25-mile march into Virginia for what will become the First Battle of Bull Run, the first major land battle of the war.
American Civil War: David Farragut is promoted to rear admiral, becoming the first officer in United States Navy to hold an admiral rank.
07-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-17.text: Nuclear weapons testing: The "Small Boy" test shot Little Feller I becomes the last atmospheric test detonation at the Nevada National Security Site.
07-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-18.text: American Civil War: Second Battle of Fort Wagner: One of the first formal African American military units, the 54th Massachusetts Volunteer Infantry, supported by several white regiments, attempts an unsuccessful assault on Confederate-held Battery Wagner.
07-18.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-18.text: U.S. Senator Ted Kennedy crashes his car into a tidal basin at Chappaquiddick Island, Massachusetts, killing his passenger, campaign specialist Mary Jo Kopechne.

07-19.text: In a private meeting with U.S. President Ronald Reagan, French President François Mitterrand reveals the existence of the Farewell Dossier, a collection of documents showing the Soviet Union had been stealing American technological research and development.

World War II: Battle of the Atlantic: German Grand Admiral Karl Dönitz orders the last U-boats to withdraw from their United States Atlantic coast positions in response to the effective American convoy system.
07-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-19.text: American Civil War: Morgan's Raid: At Buffington Island in Ohio, Confederate General John Hunt Morgan's raid into the north is mostly thwarted when a large group of his men are captured while trying to escape across the Ohio River.
07-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-20.text: The United States Department of Justice files suit in New York City against the motion picture industry charging violations of the Sherman Antitrust Act in regards to the studio system. The case would eventually result in a break-up of the industry in 1948.
In Washington, D.C., police fire tear gas on World War I veterans, part of the Bonus Expeditionary Force, who attempt to march to the White House.
American Civil War: Battle of Peachtree Creek: Near Atlanta, Georgia, Confederate forces led by General John Bell Hood unsuccessfully attack Union troops under General William T. Sherman.
07-20.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-21.text: The United States Senate ratifies the North Atlantic Treaty.
American Civil War: First Battle of Bull Run: At Manassas Junction, Virginia, the first major battle of the war begins and ends in a victory for the Confederate army.
07-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-22.text: New Deal: The United States Senate votes down President Franklin D. Roosevelt's proposal to add more justices to the Supreme Court of the United States.
07-22.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-22.text: The United States government begins compulsory civilian gasoline rationing due to the wartime demands.
07-22.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-22.text: American Civil War: Battle of Atlanta: Outside Atlanta, Confederate General John Bell Hood leads an unsuccessful attack on Union troops under General William T. Sherman on Bald Hill.
07-22.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-23.text: American Civil War: Henry Halleck takes command of the Union Army.
07-23.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-24.text: Watergate scandal: The United States Supreme Court unanimously ruled that President Richard Nixon did not have the authority to withhold subpoenaed White House tapes and they order him to surrender the tapes to the Watergate special prosecutor.
07-24.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-24.text: At the opening of the American National Exhibition in Moscow, U.S. Vice President Richard Nixon and Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev have a "Kitchen Debate".
Reconstruction: Tennessee becomes the first U.S. state to be readmitted to the Union following the American Civil War.
07-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-25.text: Vietnam War: U.S. President Richard Nixon declares the Nixon Doctrine, stating that the United States now expects its Asian allies to take care of their own military defense. This is the start of the "Vietnamization" of the war.
The United States Congress passes legislation authorizing the rank of General of the Army. Lieutenant General Ulysses S. Grant becomes the first to be promoted to this rank.
American Civil War: The United States Congress passes the Crittenden-Johnson Resolution, stating that the war is being fought to preserve the Union and not to end slavery.

07-26.text: President Harry Truman ordered the desegregation of the U.S. armed forces.
New York ratifies the United States Constitution and becomes the 11th state of the United States.
Hillary Clinton becomes the first female nominee for President of the United States by a major political party at the Democratic National Convention in Philadelphia.

07-27.text: Cessation of hostilities is achieved in the Korean War when the United States, China, and North Korea sign an armistice agreement. Syngman Rhee, President of South Korea, refuses to sign but pledges to observe the armistice.
Founding of the U.S. Army Medical Department: The Second Continental Congress passes legislation establishing "an hospital for an army consisting of 20,000 men."
Vietnam War: Five thousand more American military advisers are sent to South Vietnam bringing the total number of United States forces in Vietnam to 21,000.
07-27.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-28.text: U.S. President Herbert Hoover orders the United States Army to forcibly evict the "Bonus Army" of World War I veterans gathered in Washington, D.C.
The 14th Amendment to the United States Constitution is certified, establishing African American citizenship and guaranteeing due process of law.
At the age of 18, Vinnie Ream becomes the first and youngest female artist to receive a commission from the United States government for a statue (of Abraham Lincoln).
07-28.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

07-29.text: First United States Congress elections in Hawaii as a state of the Union.
American Civil War: Confederate spy Belle Boyd is arrested by Union troops and detained at the Old Capitol Prison in Washington, D.C.
07-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-29.text: United States and Japan sign the Harris Treaty.
07-29.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

07-30.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed Medicare and Medicaid into law.
07-30.ap_connection: Social welfare policy; Congress; federal bureaucracy

07-30.text: Watergate scandal: U.S. President Richard Nixon releases subpoenaed White House recordings after being ordered to do so by the Supreme Court of the United States.
07-30.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

07-30.text: American Civil War: Battle of the Crater: Union forces attempt to break Confederate lines at Petersburg, Virginia by exploding a large bomb under their trenches.
07-30.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

07-31.text: Dr. Patrick Francis Healy became the first African-American inaugurated as president of a predominantly white university, Georgetown University.

The U.S. Second Continental Congress passes a resolution that the services of Gilbert du Motier "be accepted, and that, in consideration of his zeal, illustrious family and connexions, he have the rank and commission of major-general of the United States."
The United States and Soviet Union both sign the START I Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty, the first to reduce (with verification) both countries' stockpiles.
07-31.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

08-01.text: Born: Ron Brown, American captain and politician, 30th United States Secretary of Commerce (d. 1996)
08-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-01.text: Born: Lawrence Eagleburger, American lieutenant and politician, 62nd United States Secretary of State (d. 2011)
08-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-01.text: Born: Robert Todd Lincoln, American lawyer and politician, 35th United States Secretary of War (d. 1926)
08-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-02.text: Calvin Coolidge became president after the death of Warren G. Harding.
The signing of the United States Declaration of Independence took place.

08-03.text: The United States Senate ratifies the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty.
Treaty of Greenville is signed, ending the Northwest Indian War in the Ohio Country.
08-03.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-04.text: U.S. President Jimmy Carter signs legislation creating the United States Department of Energy.
Civil Rights Movement: Civil rights workers Michael Schwerner, Andrew Goodman and James Chaney are found dead in Mississippi after disappearing on June 21.
08-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

08-04.text: In response to the German invasion of Belgium, Belgium and the British Empire declare war on Germany. The United States declares its neutrality.
08-04.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-05.text: The United States, the United Kingdom, and the Soviet Union sign a nuclear test ban treaty.
08-05.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

08-05.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Mobile Bay begins at Mobile Bay near Mobile, Alabama, Admiral David Farragut leads a Union flotilla through Confederate defenses and seals one of the last major Southern ports.
08-05.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-05.text: American Civil War: Battle of Baton Rouge: Along the Mississippi River near Baton Rouge, Louisiana, Confederate troops attempt to take the city, but are driven back by fire from Union gunboats.
08-05.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-06.text: President Lyndon B. Johnson signed the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
Queen Wilhelmina of the Netherlands becomes the first reigning queen to address a joint session of the United States Congress.
Sixty proof sheets of the Constitution of the United States are delivered to the Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

08-07.text: Vietnam War: The U.S. Congress passes the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution giving U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson broad war powers to deal with North Vietnamese attacks on American forces.
Canadian-born American pharmacologist Frances Oldham Kelsey awarded the U.S. President's Award for Distinguished Federal Civilian Service for her refusal to authorize thalidomide.
World War II: The Battle of Guadalcanal begins as the United States Marines initiate the first American offensive of the war with landings on Guadalcanal and Tulagi in the Solomon Islands.
08-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-08.text: President Richard Nixon, in a nationwide television address, announces his resignation from the office of the President of the United States effective noon the next day.
American Civil War: Following his defeat in the Battle of Gettysburg, General Robert E. Lee sends a letter of resignation to Confederate President Jefferson Davis (which is refused upon receipt).

08-09.text: Richard Nixon resigned the presidency, and Gerald Ford became president.
08-09.ap_connection: Presidency; checks and balances; impeachment pressure

08-09.text: World War II: Nagasaki is devastated when an atomic bomb, Fat Man, is dropped by the United States B-29 Bockscar. Thirty-five thousand people are killed outright, including 23,200-28,200 Japanese war workers, 2,000 Korean forced workers, and 150 Japanese soldiers.
08-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-09.text: American Civil War: Battle of Cedar Mountain: At Cedar Mountain, Virginia, Confederate General Stonewall Jackson narrowly defeats Union forces under General John Pope.
08-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-10.text: Japanese American internment: U.S. President Ronald Reagan signs the Civil Liberties Act of 1988, providing $20,000 payments to Japanese Americans who were either interned in or relocated by the United States during World War II.

U.S. President Harry S. Truman signs the National Security Act Amendment, streamlining the defense agencies of the United States government, and replacing the Department of War with the United States Department of Defense.
The Smithsonian Institution is chartered by the United States Congress after James Smithson donates $500,000.

08-11.text: "We begin bombing in five minutes": United States President Ronald Reagan, while running for re-election, jokes while preparing to make his weekly Saturday address on National Public Radio.
Vietnam War: The last United States ground combat unit leaves South Vietnam.
08-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-12.text: Born: Daniel Biles, American associate justice of the Kansas Supreme Court
08-12.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

08-12.text: Died: Albert Gallatin, Swiss-American ethnologist, linguist, and politician, 4th United States Secretary of the Treasury (b. 1761)
08-12.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-12.text: Born: Dale Bumpers, American soldier, lawyer, and politician, 38th Governor of Arkansas (d. 2016)
08-12.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-13.text: American Revolutionary War: The Royal Navy defeats the Penobscot Expedition with the most significant loss of United States naval forces prior to the attack on Pearl Harbor.

08-14.text: United States Senate leaders agree to rotate the office of President pro tempore of the Senate among leading candidates to fill the vacancy left by William P. Frye's death.
Oregon Territory is organized by act of Congress.

08-15.text: President Richard Nixon completes the break from the gold standard by ending convertibility of the United States dollar into gold by foreign investors.
Vietnam War: The United States bombing of Cambodia ends.
08-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-15.text: The Marquis de Lafayette, the last surviving French general of the American Revolutionary War, arrives in New York and begins a tour of 24 states.

08-16.text: U.S. President John Tyler vetoes a bill which called for the re-establishment of the Second Bank of the United States. Enraged Whig Party members riot outside the White House in the most violent demonstration on White House grounds in U.S. history.
The Migratory Bird Treaty between Canada and the United States signed.
08-16.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

08-16.text: American Revolutionary War: Battle of Camden: The British defeat the Americans near Camden, South Carolina.

08-17.text: Lewinsky scandal: US President Bill Clinton admits in taped testimony that he had an "improper physical relationship" with White House intern Monica Lewinsky; later that same day he admits before the nation that he "misled people" about the relationship.
American Civil War: Battle of Gainesville: Confederate forces defeat Union troops near Gainesville, Florida.
08-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-17.text: American Civil War: In Charleston, South Carolina, Union batteries and ships bombard Confederate-held Fort Sumter.
08-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-18.text: The 19th Amendment was ratified, prohibiting voting discrimination based on sex.
The Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, guaranteeing women's suffrage.
08-18.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

08-18.text: The Thousand Islands Bridge, connecting New York, United States with Ontario, Canada over the Saint Lawrence River, is dedicated by U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt.

08-19.text: War of 1812: American frigate USS Constitution defeats the British frigate HMS Guerriere off the coast of Nova Scotia, Canada earning the nickname "Old Ironsides".
The First Sioux War begins when United States Army soldiers kill Lakota chief Conquering Bear and in return are massacred.
08-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-19.text: American Revolutionary War: Battle of Blue Licks: The last major engagement of the war, almost ten months after the surrender of the British commander Charles Cornwallis following the Siege of Yorktown.

08-20.text: President Andrew Johnson formally declares the American Civil War over.

08-21.text: United States President Dwight D. Eisenhower signs an executive order proclaiming Hawaii the 50th state of the union. Hawaii's admission is currently commemorated by Hawaii Admission Day
American Revolutionary War: British forces begin besieging the French outpost at Pondichéry.

08-22.text: Theodore Roosevelt becomes the first President of the United States to make a public appearance in an automobile.

Alabama Chief Justice Roy Moore is suspended after refusing to comply with a federal court order to remove a rock inscribed with the Ten Commandments from the lobby of the Alabama Supreme Court building.
08-22.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

08-22.text: FBI sniper Lon Horiuchi shoots and kills Vicki Weaver during an 11-day siege at her home at Ruby Ridge, Idaho.
08-22.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-23.text: Eugene Bullard, the only black pilot in World War I, is posthumously commissioned as Second Lieutenant in the United States Air Force.
08-23.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-23.text: American Revolutionary War: King George III delivers his Proclamation of Rebellion to the Court of St James's stating that the American colonies have proceeded to a state of open and avowed rebellion.

08-24.text: British forces burned Washington, D.C., during the War of 1812.
08-24.ap_connection: National government; war powers; federal institutions

08-24.text: Buddhist crisis: As a result of the Xá Lợi Pagoda raids, the US State Department cables the United States Embassy, Saigon to encourage Army of the Republic of Vietnam generals to launch a coup against President Ngô Đình Diệm if he did not remove his brother Ngô Đình Nhu.
American Revolutionary War: A small force of Pennsylvania militia is ambushed and overwhelmed by an American Indian group, which forces George Rogers Clark to abandon his attempt to attack Detroit.

08-25.text: War of 1812: On the second day of the Burning of Washington, British troops torch the Library of Congress, United States Treasury, Department of War, and other public buildings.

08-26.text: The 19th amendment to United States Constitution takes effect, giving women the right to vote.
After John Birges plants a bomb at Harvey's Resort Hotel in Stateline, Nevada, in the United States, the FBI inadvertently detonates the bomb during its disarming.
08-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-27.text: A massive bomb planted by extortionist John Birges explodes at Harvey's Resort Hotel in Stateline, Nevada after a failed disarming attempt by the FBI. Although the hotel is damaged, no one is injured.
08-27.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-28.text: The March on Washington brought civil rights activists to the capital and featured Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' speech.
Black teenager Emmett Till is brutally murdered in Mississippi, galvanizing the nascent Civil Rights Movement.
08-28.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

08-28.text: American Civil War: Second Battle of Bull Run, also known as the Battle of Second Manassas. The battle ends on August 30.
08-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-29.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Hatteras Inlet Batteries gives Federal forces control of Pamlico Sound.
08-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-29.text: American Revolutionary War: British and American forces battle indecisively at the Battle of Rhode Island.
The United States passes the Philippine Autonomy Act.
08-29.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

08-30.text: Thurgood Marshall is confirmed as the first African American Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States.
08-30.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

08-30.text: American Civil War: Battle of Richmond: Confederates under Edmund Kirby Smith rout Union forces under General William "Bull" Nelson.
08-30.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-31.text: During the American Civil War, Union forces led by General William T. Sherman launch an assault on Atlanta.
08-31.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

08-31.text: William Livingston, the first Governor of New Jersey, begins serving his first term.
08-31.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-01.text: The United States, Australia and New Zealand sign a mutual defense pact, called the ANZUS Treaty.
09-01.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-01.text: The Fountain of Time opens as a tribute to the 100 years of peace between the United States and Great Britain following the Treaty of Ghent.
09-01.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-01.text: American Civil War: The Confederate Army General John Bell Hood orders the evacuation of Atlanta, ending a four-month siege by General William Tecumseh Sherman.
09-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-02.text: Congress created the Department of the Treasury.
Vice President of the United States Theodore Roosevelt utters the famous phrase, "Speak softly and carry a big stick" at the Minnesota State Fair.

American Civil War: United States President Abraham Lincoln reluctantly restores Union General George B. McClellan to full command after General John Pope's disastrous defeat at the Second Battle of Bull Run.

09-03.text: American Civil War: Confederate General Leonidas Polk invades neutral Kentucky, prompting the state legislature to ask for Union assistance.
American Revolutionary War: The war ends with the signing of the Treaty of Paris by the United States and the Kingdom of Great Britain.
American Revolutionary War: During the Battle of Cooch's Bridge, the Flag of the United States is flown in battle for the first time.

09-04.text: American Civil Rights Movement: Little Rock Crisis: Orval Faubus, governor of Arkansas, calls out the National Guard to prevent African American students from enrolling in Central High School.
09-04.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

09-04.text: World War II: A German submarine makes the first attack against a United States ship, the USS Greer.
09-04.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-04.text: American Civil War Maryland Campaign: General Robert E. Lee takes the Army of Northern Virginia, and the war, into the North.

09-05.text: The First Continental Congress convened in Philadelphia.
Russo-Japanese War: In New Hampshire, United States, the Treaty of Portsmouth, mediated by U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt, ends the war.
Vietnam War: Operation Jefferson Glenn begins: The United States 101st Airborne Division and the South Vietnamese 1st Infantry Division initiate a new operation in Thừa Thiên-Huế Province.
09-05.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-06.text: Leon Czolgosz, an unemployed anarchist, shoots and fatally wounds US President William McKinley at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York.
Cold War: Soviet Air Defence Forces pilot Viktor Belenko lands a Mikoyan-Gurevich MiG-25 jet fighter at Hakodate in Japan and requests political asylum in the United States; his request is granted.
09-06.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-06.text: The United States government begins the Exercise Spade Fork nuclear readiness drill.
09-06.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-07.text: The United States government takes control of the two largest mortgage financing companies in the US, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.
09-07.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-07.text: The Chrysler Corporation asks the United States government for US$1.5 billion to avoid bankruptcy.
09-07.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-07.text: Vietnam War: In a follow-up to August's Operation Starlight, United States Marines and South Vietnamese forces initiate Operation Piranha on the Batangan Peninsula.
09-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-08.text: The Northern Pacific Railway (reporting mark NP) was completed in a ceremony at Gold Creek, Montana. Former president Ulysses S. Grant drove in the final "golden spike" in an event attended by rail and political luminaries.
Cold War: United States troops arrive to partition the southern part of Korea in response to Soviet troops occupying the northern part of the peninsula a month earlier.
09-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-08.text: World War II: United States General Dwight D. Eisenhower publicly announces the Allied armistice with Italy.
09-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-09.text: Washington, D.C., the capital of the United States, is named after President George Washington.
The Continental Congress officially names its union of states the United States.
The United States Department of Housing and Urban Development is established.

09-10.text: The United States defeats the British Fleet at the Battle of Lake Erie during the War of 1812.
09-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-10.text: American Revolutionary War: Nathan Hale volunteers to spy for the Continental Army.
Lattimer massacre: A sheriff's posse kills 19 unarmed striking immigrant miners in Lattimer, Pennsylvania, United States.
09-10.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-11.text: Two hijacked aircraft crash into the World Trade Center in New York City, while a third smashes into The Pentagon in Arlington County, Virginia, and a fourth into a field near Shanksville, Pennsylvania, in a series of coordinated suicide attacks by 19 members of al-Qaeda. A total of 2,996 people are killed.
09-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-11.text: War of 1812: The climax of the Battle of Plattsburgh, a major United States victory in the war.
09-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-11.text: American Revolutionary War: Sugarloaf Massacre: A small detachment of militia from Northampton County are attacked by Native Americans and Loyalists near Little Nescopeck Creek.

09-12.text: U.S. Senator and future President John Fitzgerald Kennedy marries Jacqueline Lee Bouvier at St. Mary's Church in Newport, Rhode Island.

09-13.text: The Philadelphia Convention sets the date for the first presidential election in the United States, and New York City becomes the country's temporary capital.
Margaret Chase Smith is elected United States senator, and becomes the first woman to serve in both the U.S. House of Representatives and the United States Senate.
American Civil War: Union soldiers find a copy of Robert E. Lee's battle plans in a field outside Frederick, Maryland. It is the prelude to the Battle of Antietam.
09-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-14.text: American Civil War: The Battle of South Mountain, part of the Maryland Campaign, is fought.

09-15.text: The Senate Judiciary Committee unanimously approves Sandra Day O'Connor to become the first female justice of the Supreme Court of the United States
09-15.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

09-15.text: U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson, responding to a sniper attack at the University of Texas at Austin, writes a letter to Congress urging the enactment of gun control legislation.
Korean War: United States forces land at Inchon
09-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-16.text: American Revolutionary War: The Franco-American Siege of Savannah begins.
American Revolutionary War: The Battle of Harlem Heights is fought.

09-17.text: Delegates signed the U.S. Constitution at the Philadelphia Convention.
American Civil War: George B. McClellan halts the northward drive of Robert E. Lee's Confederate Army in the single-day Battle of Antietam, the bloodiest day in American military history.
09-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-17.text: American Civil War: The Allegheny Arsenal explosion results in the single largest civilian disaster during the war.
09-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-18.text: Margaret Chase Smith of Maine becomes the first woman elected to the United States Senate without completing another senator's term, when she defeats Democratic opponent Adrian Scolten.
The National Security Council and the Central Intelligence Agency are established in the United States under the National Security Act.
09-18.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-19.text: The Continental Congress passes the first United States federal budget.

American Civil War: Third Battle of Winchester: Union troops under General Philip Sheridan defeat a Confederate force commanded by General Jubal Early. With over 50,000 troops engaged, it was the largest battle fought in the Shenandoah Valley and was not only militarily decisive in that region of Virginia, but also played a role in securing Abraham Lincoln's election in 1864.
World War II: Battle of Hürtgen Forest between United States and Nazi Germany begins.
09-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-20.text: In an address to a joint session of Congress and the American people, U.S. President George W. Bush declares a "War on Terror".
The United States military ends its "Don't ask, don't tell" policy, allowing gay men and women to serve openly for the first time.
09-20.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-20.text: American Civil War: The conclusion of the Battle of Chickamauga in northwestern Georgia, the bloodiest two-day battle of the conflict, and the only significant Confederate victory in the war's Western Theater.
09-20.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-21.text: The Defense of Marriage Act passes the United States Congress (a vote of 342-67 in the House of Representatives and a vote of 85-14 in the Senate). The law prohibited federal recognition of same-sex marriage, while allowing states to adopt any marital definition of their choosing.
American Revolutionary War: Benedict Arnold gives the British the plans to West Point.

09-22.text: Born: J. William Middendorf, American soldier and politician, 14th United States Secretary of the Navy
09-22.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-23.text: Pilot Earle Ovington makes the first official airmail delivery in America under the authority of the United States Post Office Department
09-23.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-24.text: The Judiciary Act of 1789 established the basic structure of the federal court system.

09-25.text: Congress proposed the amendments that became the Bill of Rights.
09-25.ap_connection: Civil liberties; amendment process; Anti-Federalist concerns

09-25.text: The United States Congress establishes Sequoia National Park.

American Revolutionary War: Ethan Allen surrenders to British forces after attempting to capture Montreal during the Battle of Longue-Pointe. Benedict Arnold and his expeditionary company set off from Fort Western, bound for Quebec City.

09-26.text: The United States Federal Trade Commission (FTC) is established by the Federal Trade Commission Act.
09-26.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-26.text: Thomas Jefferson is appointed the first United States Secretary of State, John Jay is appointed the first Chief Justice of the United States, Samuel Osgood is appointed the first United States Postmaster General, and Edmund Randolph is appointed the first United States Attorney General.
09-26.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

09-27.text: The United States Department of Education receives final approval from the U.S. Congress to become the 13th US Cabinet agency.

The Continental Congress appoints John Adams to travel to France as minister plenipotentiary in charge of negotiating treaties of peace and commerce with Great Britain during the American Revolutionary War.

American Revolutionary War: Lancaster, Pennsylvania becomes the capital of the United States, for one day after the Second Continental Congress evacuates Philadelphia to avoid invading British forces.

09-28.text: The Congress of the Confederation votes to send the newly-written United States Constitution to the state legislatures for approval.
American Revolution: Samuel Huntington is elected President of the Continental Congress, succeeding John Jay.
American forces backed by a French fleet begin the siege of Yorktown, Virginia, during the American Revolutionary War.

09-29.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Chaffin's Farm is fought.
09-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-29.text: The United States Department of War first establishes a regular army with a strength of several hundred men.
09-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

09-30.text: The United States Merchant Marine Academy (USMMA) at Kings Point, New York was dedicated by President Franklin D. Roosevelt.

10-01.text: Palau gains independence from the United Nations (trusteeship administered by the United States of America).
10-01.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-01.text: The United States Defense Intelligence Agency (DIA) is formed, becoming the country's first centralized military espionage organization.
10-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-01.text: Texian political delegates convene at San Felipe de Austin to petition for changes in the governance of Mexican Texas.
10-01.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-02.text: George Washington sends proposed Constitutional amendments (The United States Bill of Rights) to the States for ratification.
Thurgood Marshall is sworn in as the first African-American justice of the United States Supreme Court.
10-02.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

10-02.text: American Civil War: Battle of Saltville: Union forces attack Saltville, Virginia, but are defeated by Confederate troops.
10-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-03.text: George Washington issued the first presidential Thanksgiving proclamation.
The last Thursday in November is declared as Thanksgiving Day by United States President Abraham Lincoln as are Thursdays, November 30, 1865 and November 29, 1866.

10-04.text: Russian Constitutional Crisis: In Moscow, tanks bombard the White House, a government building that housed the Russian parliament, while demonstrators against President Boris Yeltsin rally outside.

The second largest cash robbery in U.S. history occurs at the Charlotte, North Carolina office of Loomis, Fargo and Company. A Federal Bureau of Investigation investigation eventually results in 24 convictions and the recovery of approximately 95% of the $17.3 million stolen cash.

10-05.text: The first televised White House address is given by U.S. President Harry S. Truman.

10-06.text: World War II: The October Matanikau action on Guadalcanal begins as United States Marine Corps forces attack Imperial Japanese Army units along the Matanikau River.
10-06.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-06.text: The Naval War College of the United States Navy is founded in Newport, Rhode Island.
10-06.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-06.text: American Revolutionary War: General Sir Henry Clinton leads British forces in the capture of Continental Army Hudson River defenses in the Battle of Forts Clinton and Montgomery.

10-07.text: World War II: The October Matanikau action on Guadalcanal begins as United States Marine Corps forces attack Imperial Japanese Army units along the Matanikau River.
10-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-07.text: World War II: The McCollum memo proposes bringing the United States into the war in Europe by provoking the Japanese to attack the United States.
10-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-07.text: American Civil War: Bahia incident: USS Wachusett illegally captures the CSS Florida Confederate raider while in port in Bahia, Brazil in violation of Brazilian neutrality.
10-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-08.text: World War I: In the Argonne Forest in France, United States Corporal Alvin C. York kills 28 German soldiers and captures 132, for which he is awarded the Medal of Honor.
10-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-08.text: American Civil War: Battle of Perryville: Union forces under General Don Carlos Buell halt the Confederate invasion of Kentucky by defeating troops led by General Braxton Bragg at Perryville, Kentucky.
10-08.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-09.text: American Civil War: Battle of Tom's Brook: Union cavalrymen in the Shenandoah Valley defeat Confederate forces at Toms Brook, Virginia.
10-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-09.text: American Civil War: Battle of Santa Rosa Island: Union troops repel a Confederate attempt to capture Fort Pickens.
10-09.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-10.text: Vice President of the United States Spiro Agnew resigns after being charged with evasion of federal income tax.
United States President Woodrow Wilson triggers the explosion of the Gamboa Dike, ending construction on the Panama Canal.
A Mutual Defense Treaty Between the United States and the Republic of Korea is concluded in Washington, D.C.
10-10.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-11.text: World War II: Battle of Cape Esperance: On the northwest coast of Guadalcanal, United States Navy ships intercept and defeat a Japanese fleet on their way to reinforce troops on the island.
10-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-11.text: American Civil War: In the aftermath of the Battle of Antietam, Confederate General J. E. B. Stuart and his men loot Chambersburg, Pennsylvania, during a raid into the north.
10-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-11.text: American Revolutionary War: Battle of Valcour Island: On Lake Champlain a fleet of American boats is defeated by the Royal Navy, but delays the British advance until 1777.

10-12.text: The USS Cole attack later shaped debates over presidential war powers and national security policy.
10-12.ap_connection: Foreign policy; executive power; national security

10-12.text: Vietnam War: US President Richard Nixon announces that the United States will withdraw 40,000 more troops before Christmas
President Theodore Roosevelt officially renames the "Executive Mansion" to the White House.

10-13.text: A majority of voters in the Republic of Texas approve a proposed constitution that, if accepted by the U.S. Congress, will make Texas a U.S. state.
The United States Continental Congress orders the establishment of the Continental Navy (the predecessor organization of the United States Navy).

War of 1812: Battle of Queenston Heights: As part of the Niagara campaign in Ontario, Canada, United States forces under General Stephen Van Rensselaer are repulsed from invading Canada by British and native troops led by Sir Isaac Brock.

10-14.text: While campaigning in Milwaukee, the former President of the United States, Theodore Roosevelt, is shot and mildly wounded by John Schrank, a mentally-disturbed saloon keeper. With the fresh wound in his chest, and the bullet still within it, Mr. Roosevelt still carries out his scheduled public speech.

Vietnam War: The United States Department of Defense announces that the U.S. Army and U.S. Marine Corps will send about 24,000 soldiers and Marines back to Vietnam for involuntary second tours of duty in the combat zone there.
10-14.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-14.text: American Civil War: Battle of Bristoe Station: Confederate troops under the command of General Robert E. Lee fail to drive the Union Army completely out of Virginia.
10-14.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-15.text: The Senate confirmed Clarence Thomas to the Supreme Court after highly contested hearings.
10-15.ap_connection: Judicial appointments; Senate confirmation; public opinion

10-15.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Glasgow is fought, resulting in the surrender of Glasgow, Missouri and its Union garrison, to the Confederacy.
10-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-15.text: American Civil War: The H. L. Hunley, the first submarine to sink a ship, sinks during a test, killing its inventor, Horace L. Hunley.
10-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-16.text: Royalton, Vermont and Tunbridge, Vermont are the last major raids of the American Revolutionary War.

10-17.text: American Revolutionary War: British General Charles, Earl Cornwallis surrenders at the Siege of Yorktown.
American Revolutionary War: British General John Burgoyne surrenders his army at Saratoga, New York.

10-18.text: American Revolutionary War: The Franco-American Siege of Savannah is lifted.
American Revolutionary War: The Burning of Falmouth (now Portland, Maine).

10-19.text: British surrender at Yorktown effectively ended major fighting in the American Revolution.
Cold War: The United States government imposes a near-total trade embargo against Cuba.
10-19.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-19.text: John Jay is sworn in as the first Chief Justice of the United States.
10-19.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-20.text: "Saturday Night Massacre": United States President Richard Nixon fires U.S. Attorney General Elliot Richardson and Deputy Attorney General William Ruckelshaus after they refuse to fire Watergate special prosecutor Archibald Cox, who is finally fired by Robert Bork.
The United States Senate ratifies the Louisiana Purchase.
The Convention of 1818 is signed between the United States and the United Kingdom, which settles the Canada-United States border on the 49th parallel for most of its length.
10-20.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-21.text: U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower signs an executive order transferring Wernher von Braun and other German scientists from the United States Army to NASA.
In Boston Harbor, the 44-gun United States Navy frigate USS Constitution is launched.
American Civil War: Battle of Ball's Bluff: Union forces under Colonel Edward Baker are defeated by Confederate troops in the second major battle of the war.
10-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-22.text: Cuban Missile Crisis: US President John F. Kennedy, after internal counsel from Dwight D. Eisenhower, announces that American reconnaissance planes have discovered Soviet nuclear weapons in Cuba, and that he has ordered a naval "quarantine" of the Communist nation.
The United States Federal Labor Relations Authority votes to decertify the Professional Air Traffic Controllers Organization (PATCO) for its strike the previous August.
Vietnam War: First United States casualties in Vietnam.
10-22.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-23.text: Vietnam War: The 1st Cavalry Division (United States) (Airmobile), in conjunction with South Vietnamese forces, launches a new operation seeking to destroy North Vietnamese forces in Pleiku in the II Corps Tactical Zone (the Central Highlands).
10-23.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-23.text: American Civil War: Battle of Westport: Union forces under General Samuel R. Curtis defeat Confederate troops led by General Sterling Price at Westport, Missouri, near Kansas City.
10-23.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-23.text: Women's suffrage: In New York City, 25,000-33,000 women march on Fifth Avenue to advocate their right to vote.
10-23.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

10-24.text: Born: James S. Sherman, American lawyer and politician, 27th Vice President of the United States (d. 1912)
Died: Elias Boudinot, American lawyer and politician, 10th President of the Continental Congress (b. 1740)
Died: Winton M. Blount, American soldier and politician, 59th United States Postmaster General (b. 1921)
10-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-25.text: War of 1812: The American frigate, USS United States, commanded by Stephen Decatur, captures the British frigate HMS Macedonian.
10-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-26.text: The USA PATRIOT Act was signed, expanding federal surveillance and law-enforcement powers after September 11.
The first Continental Congress adjourns in Philadelphia.
The United States passes the USA PATRIOT Act into law.
10-26.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-27.text: United States Navy radioman Allen R. Schindler, Jr. is murdered by shipmate Terry M. Helvey for being gay, precipitating debate about gays in the military that resulted in the United States "Don't ask, don't tell" military policy.
10-27.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-27.text: The United States and Spain sign the Treaty of Madrid, which establishes the boundaries between Spanish colonies and the U.S.
10-27.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-27.text: Missouri governor Lilburn Boggs issues the Extermination Order, which orders all Mormons to leave the state or be exterminated.
10-27.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

10-28.text: American Civil War: The Battle of Fair Oaks & Darbytown Road (also known as the Second Battle of Fair Oaks) ends: Union forces under General Ulysses S. Grant withdraw from Fair Oaks, Virginia, after failing to breach the Confederate defenses around Richmond, Virginia.
10-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-28.text: American Revolutionary War: Battle of White Plains: British Army forces arrive at White Plains, attack and capture Chatterton Hill from the Americans.
American Revolutionary War: A British proclamation forbids residents from leaving Boston.

10-29.text: Francisco Martin Duran fires over two dozen shots at the White House; he is later convicted of trying to kill US President Bill Clinton.

American Civil War: Battle of Wauhatchie: Forces under Union General Ulysses S. Grant repel a Confederate attack led by General James Longstreet. Union forces thus open a supply line into Chattanooga, Tennessee.
10-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-30.text: Cold War: U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower formally approves the top secret document National Security Council Paper No. 162/2, which states that the United States' arsenal of nuclear weapons must be maintained and expanded to counter the communist threat.
Vietnam War: United States Marines repel an intense attack by Viet Cong forces, killing 56 guerrillas near Da Nang.
10-30.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-30.text: Orson Welles broadcasts his radio play of H. G. Wells's The War of the Worlds, causing anxiety in some of the audience in the United States.
10-30.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-31.text: World War II: An F4U Corsair accomplishes the first successful radar-guided interception by a United States Navy or Marine Corps aircraft.
10-31.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-31.text: American Civil War: Citing failing health, Union General Winfield Scott resigns as Commander of the United States Army.
10-31.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

10-31.text: A federal grand jury in Houston, Texas indicts former Enron chief financial officer Andrew Fastow on 78 counts of wire fraud, money laundering, conspiracy and obstruction of justice related to the collapse of his ex-employer.
10-31.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-01.text: While campaigning for President of the United States, John F. Kennedy announces his idea of the Peace Corps.
American Civil War: U.S. President Abraham Lincoln appoints George B. McClellan as the commander of the Union Army, replacing General Winfield Scott.
John Adams becomes the first President of the United States to live in the Executive Mansion (later renamed the White House).

11-02.text: In the United States, KDKA of Pittsburgh starts broadcasting as the first commercial radio station. The first broadcast is the result of the United States presidential election, 1920.
Norman Morrison, a 31-year-old Quaker, sets himself on fire in front of the river entrance to the Pentagon to protest the use of napalm in the Vietnam war.
11-02.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-02.text: Vietnam War: US President Lyndon B. Johnson and "The Wise Men" conclude that the American people should be given more optimistic reports on the progress of the war.

11-03.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt is re-elected President of the United States.
William Howard Taft is elected the 27th President of the United States.
John Willis Menard (R-Louisiana) was the first African American elected to the United States Congress. Because of an electoral challenge, he was never seated.

11-04.text: Barack Obama was elected president, becoming the first Black president of the United States.
Salvador Allende takes office as President of Chile, the first Marxist to become president of a Latin American country through open elections.
Ronald Reagan is elected the 40th President of The United States, defeating incumbent Jimmy Carter.

11-05.text: Franklin D. Roosevelt is the first and only President of the United States to be elected to a third term.
Women's suffrage in the United States: In defiance of the law, suffragist Susan B. Anthony votes for the first time, and is later fined $100.
11-05.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

11-05.text: American Civil War: Abraham Lincoln removes George B. McClellan as commander of the Army of the Potomac.
11-05.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-06.text: Barack Obama is reelected President of the United States; Tammy Baldwin becomes the first openly gay politician to be elected to the United States Senate.
Ronald Reagan is reelected President of the United States.
Dwight D. Eisenhower is reelected President of the United States.

11-07.text: Jeannette Rankin became the first woman elected to Congress.
Controversial US presidential election that is later resolved in the Bush v. Gore Supreme Court Case, electing George W. Bush the 43rd President of the United States.
11-07.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

11-07.text: The United States Congress overrides President Richard M. Nixon's veto of the War Powers Resolution, which limits presidential power to wage war without congressional approval.

11-08.text: John F. Kennedy was elected president in one of the closest elections in U.S. history.
Donald Trump is elected 45th President of the United States defeating Hillary Clinton. She is the fifth presidential candidate in U.S. history to win the popular vote but lose the election.

Republican Revolution: On the night of the 1994 United States midterm elections, Republicans make historic electoral gains by securing massive majorities in both houses of congress (54 seats in the House and eight seats in the Senate, additionally). Thus bringing a close to four decades of Democratic domination.

11-09.text: The fall of the Berlin Wall reshaped U.S. foreign policy debates at the end of the Cold War.

Vietnam War: The Supreme Court of the United States votes 6-3 against hearing a case to allow Massachusetts to enforce its law granting residents the right to refuse military service in an undeclared war.
11-09.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

11-09.text: Theodore Roosevelt is the first sitting President of the United States to make an official trip outside the country. He did so to inspect progress on the Panama Canal.

11-10.text: Beginning of the Wilmington insurrection of 1898, the only instance of a municipal government being overthrown in United States history.
11-10.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

11-10.text: Major Henry Wirz, the superintendent of a prison camp in Andersonville, Georgia, is hanged, becoming one of only three American Civil War soldiers executed for war crimes.
11-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-10.text: The last colonial governor of New Jersey, William Franklin, signs the charter of Queen's College (later renamed Rutgers University).
11-10.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

11-11.text: Vietnam War: Vietnamization: The United States Army turns over the massive Long Binh military base to South Vietnam.
11-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-11.text: American Civil War: General William Tecumseh Sherman begins burning Atlanta to the ground in preparation for his march to the sea.
11-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-11.text: Cherry Valley massacre: Loyalists and Seneca Indian forces attack a fort and village in eastern New York during the American Revolutionary War, killing more than forty civilians and soldiers.

11-12.text: Iran hostage crisis: In response to the hostage situation in Tehran, US President Jimmy Carter orders a halt to all petroleum imports into the United States from Iran.
Vietnam War: As part of Vietnamization, US President Richard Nixon sets February 1, 1972 as the deadline for the removal of another 45,000 American troops from Vietnam.

11-13.text: War on Terror: In the first such act since World War II, US President George W. Bush signs an executive order allowing military tribunals against foreigners suspected of connections to terrorist acts or planned acts on the United States.
The Supreme Court of the United States declares Alabama laws requiring segregated buses illegal, thus ending the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
11-13.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

11-13.text: The Compact of Free Association becomes law, granting the Federated States of Micronesia, the Marshall Islands independence from the United States.
11-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-14.text: Iran hostage crisis: US President Jimmy Carter issues Executive order 12170, freezing all Iranian assets in the United States in response to the hostage crisis.
American Civil War: President Abraham Lincoln approves General Ambrose Burnside's plan to capture the Confederate capital at Richmond, Virginia, leading to the Battle of Fredericksburg.

11-15.text: American Revolutionary War: After 16 months of debate the Continental Congress approves the Articles of Confederation.
American Civil War: Union General William Tecumseh Sherman begins Sherman's March to the Sea.
11-15.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-16.text: The Federal Reserve Bank of the United States officially opens.
11-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-16.text: Indian Territory and Oklahoma Territory join to form Oklahoma, which is admitted as the 46th U.S. state.
11-16.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

11-16.text: American Civil War: Battle of Campbell's Station near Knoxville, Tennessee: Confederate troops unsuccessfully attack Union forces.
11-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-17.text: The United States Congress holds its first session in Washington, D.C.
Articles of Confederation (United States) are submitted to the states for ratification.
American Civil War: Siege of Knoxville begins: Confederate forces led by General James Longstreet place Knoxville, Tennessee, under siege.
11-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-18.text: Susan B. Anthony and 14 other women are arrested for illegal voting in the United States presidential election of 1872.

The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court rules 4-3 in Goodridge v. Department of Public Health that the state's ban on same-sex marriage is unconstitutional and gives the state legislature 180 days to change the law making Massachusetts the first state in the United States to grant marriage rights to same-sex couples.
11-18.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

11-18.text: United States President John F. Kennedy sends 18,000 military advisors to South Vietnam.[citation needed]

11-19.text: Abraham Lincoln delivered the Gettysburg Address, redefining the Civil War around equality and democratic government.
11-19.ap_connection: Founding ideals; popular sovereignty; civil rights

11-19.text: Lewinsky scandal: The United States House of Representatives Judiciary Committee begins impeachment hearings against U.S. President Bill Clinton.
The United States and the Kingdom of Great Britain sign Jay's Treaty, which attempts to resolve some of the lingering problems left over from the American Revolutionary War.

11-20.text: New Jersey becomes the first U.S. state to ratify the Bill of Rights.
Savings and loan crisis: The United States Senate Ethics Committee issues a stern censure of California senator Alan Cranston for his "dealings" with savings-and-loan executive Charles Keating.
The United States Department of Justice files its final anti-trust suit against AT&T Corporation. This suit later leads to the breakup of AT&T and its Bell System.

11-21.text: North Carolina ratifies the United States Constitution and is admitted as the 12th U.S. state.
American Civil War: Confederate President Jefferson Davis appoints Judah Benjamin Secretary of War.
Vietnam War: Operation Ivory Coast: A joint United States Air Force and Army team raids the Sơn Tây prisoner-of-war camp in an attempt to free American prisoners of war thought to be held there.
11-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-22.text: President John F. Kennedy was assassinated, and Lyndon B. Johnson became president.
11-22.ap_connection: Presidential succession; executive branch; public trust

11-22.text: American Civil War: John Bell Hood begins the Franklin-Nashville Campaign in an unsuccessful attempt to draw William Tecumseh Sherman back out of Georgia.

11-23.text: American Civil War: Battle of Chattanooga begins: Union forces led by General Ulysses S. Grant reinforce troops at Chattanooga, Tennessee, and counter-attack Confederate troops.
11-23.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-24.text: World War II: The United States grants Lend-Lease to the Free French Forces.
11-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-24.text: American Civil War: Battle of Lookout Mountain: Near Chattanooga, Tennessee, Union forces under General Ulysses S. Grant capture Lookout Mountain and begin to break the Confederate siege of the city led by General Braxton Bragg.
11-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-24.text: The Texas Provincial Government authorizes the creation of a horse-mounted police force called the Texas Rangers (which is now the Texas Ranger Division of the Texas Department of Public Safety).
11-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-25.text: The United States Greenback Party is established as a political party consisting primarily of farmers affected by the Panic of 1873.

American Civil War: A group of Confederate operatives calling themselves the Confederate Army of Manhattan starts fires in more than 20 locations in an unsuccessful attempt to burn down New York City.
11-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-25.text: American Civil War: Battle of Missionary Ridge: At Missionary Ridge in Tennessee, Union forces led by General Ulysses S. Grant break the Siege of Chattanooga by routing Confederate troops under General Braxton Bragg.
11-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-26.text: George W. Bush is certified the winner of Florida's electoral votes by Katherine Harris, going on to win the United States presidential election, despite losing in the national popular vote.

United States President Abraham Lincoln proclaims November 26 as a national Thanksgiving Day, to be celebrated annually on the final Thursday of November. (Since 1941, it has been on the fourth Thursday.)
A national Thanksgiving Day is observed in the United States as proclaimed by President George Washington at the request of Congress.

11-27.text: Vietnam War: The Pentagon tells U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson that if planned operations are to succeed, the number of American troops in Vietnam has to be increased from 120,000 to 400,000.
Twenty-fifth Amendment: The United States Senate votes 92-3 to confirm Gerald Ford as Vice President of the United States. (On December 6, the House will confirm him 387-35).
American Civil War: Confederate cavalry leader John Hunt Morgan and several of his men escape the Ohio Penitentiary and return safely to the South.
11-27.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-28.text: American Civil War: In the Battle of Cane Hill, Union troops under General James G. Blunt defeat General John Marmaduke's Confederates.
11-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-29.text: American Civil War: Battle of Spring Hill: A Confederate advance into Tennessee misses an opportunity to crush the Union Army. General John Bell Hood is angered, which leads to the Battle of Franklin.
11-29.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-29.text: American Revolutionary War: The Battle of Fort Cumberland, Nova Scotia, comes to an end with the arrival of British reinforcements.

11-30.text: The Democratic-Republican-controlled United States Senate begins an impeachment trial of Federalist Supreme Court Justice Samuel Chase.
11-30.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation
