11-30.text: Cold War: In Geneva, representatives from the United States and the Soviet Union begin to negotiate intermediate-range nuclear weapon reductions in Europe. (The meetings end inconclusively on December 17.)
11-30.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

11-30.text: Vietnam War: White House Press Secretary Ron Ziegler tells the press that there will be no more public announcements concerning American troop withdrawals from Vietnam because troop levels are now down to 27,000.

12-01.text: Rosa Parks was arrested in Montgomery, Alabama, helping spark the Montgomery Bus Boycott.
12-01.ap_connection: Civil rights; social movements; equal protection

12-01.text: United States presidential election, 1824: Since no candidate received a majority of the total electoral college votes in the election, the United States House of Representatives is given the task of deciding the winner in accordance with the Twelfth Amendment to the United States Constitution.
Vietnam War: The first draft lottery in the United States is held since World War II.
12-01.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-02.text: Manifest destiny: In a State of the Union message, U.S. President James K. Polk proposes that the United States should aggressively expand into the West.

Vietnam War: After a trip to Vietnam at the request of U.S. President John F. Kennedy, U.S. Senate Majority Leader Mike Mansfield becomes the first American official to comment adversely on the war's progress.
Monroe Doctrine: In a State of the Union message, U.S. President James Monroe proclaims American neutrality in future European conflicts, and warns European powers not to interfere in the Americas.

12-03.text: In Ottawa, Ontario, Canada, representatives from 121 countries sign the Ottawa Treaty prohibiting manufacture and deployment of anti-personnel landmines. The United States, People's Republic of China, and Russia do not sign the treaty, however.
12-03.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-04.text: World War II: U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt closes down the Works Progress Administration, because of the high levels of wartime employment in the United States.
By a vote of 65-7, the United States Senate approves United States participation in the United Nations. (The UN had been established on October 24, 1945.)

American Civil War: Sherman's March to the Sea: At Waynesboro, Georgia, forces under Union General Judson Kilpatrick prevent troops led by Confederate General Joseph Wheeler from interfering with Union General William T. Sherman's campaign destroying a wide swath of the South on his march to the Atlantic Ocean from Atlanta.

12-05.text: The 21st Amendment was ratified, repealing Prohibition.
The Twenty-first Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified.
California Gold Rush: In a message to the United States Congress, U.S. President James K. Polk confirms that large amounts of gold had been discovered in California.

12-06.text: The Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution is ratified, banning slavery.

The Twenty-fifth Amendment: The United States House of Representatives votes 387 to 35 to confirm Gerald Ford as Vice President of the United States. (On November 27, the Senate confirmed him 92 to 3.)
The U.S. Congress moves from New York City to Philadelphia.

12-07.text: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, leading Congress to declare war the next day.
Delaware becomes the first state to ratify the United States Constitution.
World War I: The United States declares war on Austria-Hungary.
12-07.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-08.text: U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower delivers his "Atoms for Peace" speech, which leads to an American program to supply equipment and information on nuclear power to schools, hospitals, and research institutions around the world.

12-09.text: American Civil War: The Joint Committee on the Conduct of the War is established by the U.S. Congress.
The Governor of Illinois, Rod Blagojevich, is arrested by federal officials for crimes including attempting to sell the U.S. Senate seat being vacated by President-elect Barack Obama.
In Louisiana, P. B. S. Pinchback becomes the first African-American governor of a U.S. state.
12-09.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-10.text: Wyoming Territory granted women the right to vote, decades before the 19th Amendment.
U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt wins the Nobel Peace Prize for his role in the mediation of the Russo-Japanese War, becoming the first American to win a Nobel Prize.
American Civil War: Sherman's March to the Sea: Major General William Tecumseh Sherman's Union Army troops reach the outer Confederate defenses of Savannah, Georgia.
12-10.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-11.text: The International Conference to Review the Global Vision of the Holocaust is opened in Tehran, Iran, by then-president Mahmoud Ahmadinejad; nations such as Israel and the United States express concern.

World War II: Germany and Italy declare war on the United States, following the Americans' declaration of war on the Empire of Japan in the wake of the attack on Pearl Harbor. The United States, in turn, declares war on them.
12-11.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-11.text: The U.S. Senate creates a select committee on finance and a uniform national currency, predecessor of the United States Senate Committee on Finance.

12-12.text: Pennsylvania becomes the second state to ratify the United States Constitution, five days after Delaware became the first.
The United States Supreme Court releases its decision in Bush v. Gore.
12-12.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

12-12.text: World War II: The United Kingdom declares war on Bulgaria. Hungary and Romania declare war on the United States. India declares war on Japan.
12-12.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-13.text: American Civil War: At the Battle of Fredericksburg, Confederate General Robert E. Lee defeats Union Major General Ambrose Burnside.
12-13.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-14.text: American Civil Rights Movement: Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States: The Supreme Court of the United States rules that Congress can use the Constitution's Commerce Clause to fight discrimination.
12-14.ap_connection: Supreme Court; judicial power; constitutional interpretation

12-15.text: The Bill of Rights was ratified, protecting key civil liberties against the national government.
12-15.ap_connection: Bill of Rights; civil liberties; amendments

12-15.text: U.S. President Jimmy Carter announces that the United States will recognize the People's Republic of China and sever diplomatic relations with Taiwan
The Twenty-first Amendment to the United States Constitution officially becomes effective, repealing the Eighteenth Amendment that prohibited the sale, manufacture, and transportation of alcohol.

12-16.text: American Civil War: Battle of Nashville: Major General George Thomas's Union forces defeat Lieutenant General John Bell Hood's Confederate Army of Tennessee.
12-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-16.text: American Civil War: Joseph E. Johnston replaces Braxton Bragg as commander of the Confederate Army of Tennessee.
12-16.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-17.text: The American Civil Rights Congress delivers "We Charge Genocide" to the United Nations.
12-17.ap_connection: Civil rights; equal protection; federal action

12-17.text: American Civil War: General Ulysses S. Grant issues General Order No. 11, expelling Jews from parts of Tennessee, Mississippi, and Kentucky.
12-17.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-18.text: The 13th Amendment was ratified, abolishing slavery in the United States.
12-18.ap_connection: Civil rights; Reconstruction amendments; federal power

12-18.text: New Jersey becomes the third state to ratify the U.S. Constitution.

Vietnam War: President Richard Nixon announces that the United States will engage North Vietnam in Operation Linebacker II, a series of Christmas bombings, after peace talks collapsed with North Vietnam on the 13th.

12-19.text: The House of Representatives impeached President Bill Clinton.
Nelson Rockefeller is sworn in as Vice President of the United States under President Gerald Ford under the provisions of the 25th Amendment to the United States Constitution.
Nullification Crisis: Vice President of the United States John C. Calhoun pens the South Carolina Exposition and Protest, protesting the Tariff of 1828.

12-20.text: The United States invasion of Panama deposes Manuel Noriega.
12-20.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-21.text: Born: Donald Regan, American colonel and politician, 11th White House Chief of Staff (d. 2003)
Born: John William McCormack, American lawyer and politician, 53rd Speaker of the United States House of Representatives (d. 1980)
Died: Frank B. Kellogg, American lawyer and politician, 45th United States Secretary of State, Nobel Prize laureate (b. 1856)
12-21.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-22.text: The repeal of the Don't ask, don't tell policy, the 17-year-old policy banning homosexuals serving openly in the United States military, is signed into law by President Barack Obama.
World War II: Battle of the Bulge: German troops demand the surrender of United States troops at Bastogne, Belgium, prompting the famous one word reply by General Anthony McAuliffe: "Nuts!"
12-22.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-23.text: The Federal Reserve Act is signed into law by President Woodrow Wilson, creating the Federal Reserve System.

12-24.text: A Canadair CL-44 chartered by the United States military crashes into a small village in South Vietnam, killing 129.
12-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-24.text: The Eggnog Riot at the United States Military Academy begins that night, wrapping up the following morning.
12-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-24.text: Representatives of Britain and the United States sign the Treaty of Ghent, ending the War of 1812.
12-24.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-25.text: United States President Andrew Johnson grants an unconditional pardon to all Confederate veterans.
The Eggnog Riot at the United States Military Academy concludes after beginning the previous evening.
12-25.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-26.text: U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt signs a bill establishing the fourth Thursday in November as Thanksgiving Day in the United States.
A theater fire in Richmond, Virginia kills the Governor of Virginia George William Smith and the president of the First National Bank of Virginia Abraham B. Venable.
American Civil War: The Battle of Chickasaw Bayou begins.
12-26.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-27.text: Born: Louis Lincoln Emmerson, American lawyer and politician, 27th Governor of Illinois (d. 1941)
12-27.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-28.text: John C. Calhoun becomes the first Vice President of the United States to resign.
United States claims Midway Atoll, the first territory annexed outside Continental limits.
12-28.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-28.text: Osceola leads his Seminole warriors in Florida into the Second Seminole War against the United States Army.
12-28.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-29.text: The Treaty of New Echota is signed, ceding all the lands of the Cherokee east of the Mississippi River to the United States.
12-29.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-29.text: American Revolutionary War: Three thousand British soldiers under the command of Lieutenant Colonel Archibald Campbell capture Savannah, Georgia.

12-30.text: Vietnam War: The United States halts heavy bombing of North Vietnam.
12-30.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties

12-30.text: The Treaty of St. Louis between the United States and the Shawnee Nation is proclaimed.
12-30.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-30.text: The Treaty of St. Louis (1816) between the United States and the united Ottawa, Ojibwa, and Potawatomi Indian tribes is proclaimed.
12-30.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-31.text: The United States Government hands control of the Panama Canal (as well all the adjacent land to the canal known as the Panama Canal Zone) to Panama. This act complied with the signing of the 1977 Torrijos-Carter Treaties.
12-31.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-31.text: The AT&T Bell System is broken up by the United States Government.
12-31.ap_connection: American political development; institutions; public policy

12-31.text: American Civil War: Abraham Lincoln signs an act that admits West Virginia to the Union, thus dividing Virginia in two.
12-31.ap_connection: National security; executive power; civil liberties
